OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of human immunodeficiency virus and hepatitis C virus coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones. Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Nail–patella syndrome, Coffin–Siris syndrome, Meier–Gorlin syndrome, Coxopodopatellar syndrome, Genitopatellar syndrome
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Bilateral hypoplastic fingernails on physical exam.  
- Bilateral ovoid osseous processes projecting from posterior iliac bones on CT.  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation on knee radiographs.  
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature on elbow radiographs.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome:  
   - Pros: Classic triad includes nail hypoplasia/dysplasia, bilateral patellar hypoplasia/aplasia, and iliac horns (posterior iliac osseous processes). The presence of radial head hypoplasia and elbow dislocation has been reported.  
   - Cons: None significant; the findings are highly characteristic.  

2) Coffin–Siris syndrome:  
   - Pros: Features may include nail hypoplasia.  
   - Cons: Usually presents with hypertrichosis, coarse facial features, and intellectual disability, but does not typically involve patellar hypoplasia or iliac horns.  

3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome:  
   - Pros: Can show radial hypoplasia and patent patella.  
   - Cons: Features include short stature, microtia, and patellar agenesis, but no nail hypoplasia or iliac horns. The combination here is less typical.  

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:  
   - Pros: Patellar hypoplasia/agenesis and elbow dislocations are common.  
   - Cons: Nail anomalies are not a defining feature. Iliac horns are not classically described. Typically includes severe proximal limb and pelvic anomalies with distinctive facial features.  

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:  
   - Pros: Patellar aplasia/hypoplasia and genital anomalies.  
   - Cons: Nail hypoplasia and iliac horns are not hallmarks. Usually associated with agenesis of corpus callosum and severe developmental delay.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome: Fits classic triad (nail hypoplasia, patellar hypoplasia, iliac horns) with additional elbow abnormalities.  
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome: Similar orthopedic findings but lacks nail hypoplasia and iliac horns.  
3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome: Shares some features but lacks nail hypoplasia and iliac horns.  
4) Coffin–Siris syndrome: Nail hypoplasia present, but patellar and iliac findings less typical.  
5) Genitopatellar syndrome: Less compatibility with nail and iliac findings.  

<final>  
Nail–patella syndrome  
</final><|return|>
options: Nail–patella syndrome | Coffin–Siris syndrome | Meier–Gorlin syndrome | Coxopodopatellar syndrome | Genitopatellar syndrome

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe. A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Elderly male (81 years) with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and new onset orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea.  
- Labs: elevated troponins; ECG: third-degree AV block.  
- Echocardiogram: right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.  
- PET-CT: large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue involving right-sided chambers (right atrium, right ventricle), interatrial septum, and around large vessels, sparing the left ventricle; hypermetabolic nodular image in left lung base.  
- Contrast-enhanced CT: infiltrative hypodense mass causing mural thickening of right cardiac cavities; cuff around right coronary artery; moderate pericardial effusion.  
- Lung nodule confirmed solid and biopsied.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Angiosarcoma  
   Pros: Often occurs in right atrium and can infiltrate myocardium; can cause pericardial effusion; highly FDG-avid.  
   Cons: Typically more locally aggressive but may not spare left ventricle entirely; lung metastasis possible but not always nodular and FDG-avid; less common to involve interventricular septum extensively and to encircle coronary arteries.  

2) Ig-G4 related disease  
   Pros: Can cause infiltrative soft tissue masses around vessels and pericardial effusion; some cases involve myocardium.  
   Cons: Generally presents with diffuse fibrosis rather than focal hypermetabolic mass; PET-CT usually shows low to moderate FDG uptake; rarely causes large myocardial masses with extensive infiltration; histology would show IgG4 plasma cells, but imaging findings here more aggressive; patient's age and presentation less typical.  

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma  
   Pros: Typically involves right heart chambers (right atrium, right ventricle), interatrial septum often involved; can cause pericardial effusion; highly FDG avid; can encase coronary arteries; can present with conduction abnormalities (AV block); lung metastasis or nodules can occur; age fits median 60-70 years; aggressive infiltrative mass sparing left ventricle fits well.  
   Cons: Less common but very plausible given the pattern.  

4) Erdheim–Chester disease  
   Pros: Can infiltrate myocardium and show pericardial effusion; FDG avid.  
   Cons: Typically associated with right atrial pseudotumor but also involves long bones and other systemic bones; not primarily a myocardial infiltrative mass with nodular lung lesion; age typically 45-60; less likely to cause AV block or massive right-sided myocardial infiltration; lung involvement usually interstitial, not nodular.  

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS)  
   Pros: Can cause large infiltrative cardiac masses, often in left atrium but can involve any chamber; can be FDG avid.  
   Cons: Usually primary left-sided; less common right-sided infiltration and interatrial septum involvement; rarely encases coronary arteries; lung metastasis possible but not typical early nodular lung lesion; age can fit but presentation less typical given sparing of left ventricle and nodal lung involvement.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Primary cardiac lymphoma - matches right atrial and right ventricle involvement, interatrial septum, pericardial effusion, conduction block, FDG avid mass encasing coronary artery, lung nodule likely metastasis or site of disease; classic presentation.  
2) Angiosarcoma - right atrial involvement and pericardial effusion, FDG avid, but less typical for interatrial septum involvement and coronary artery cuffing; lung nodule possible metastasis but nodules less common than diffuse metastases.  
3) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma - less typical right-sided and interventricular septum involvement; lung nodule possible metastasis but pattern less classic.  
4) Erdheim–Chester disease - does involve heart but usually more fibrotic, systemic bone disease absent, presentation less acute.  
5) Ig-G4 related disease - less likely given aggressive infiltrative mass and FDG avidity.  

<final>Primary cardiac lymphoma</final><|return|>
options: Angiosarcoma | Ig-G4 related disease | Primary cardiac lymphoma | Erdheim–Chester disease | Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 37-year-old male with acute febrile illness (high-grade fever, chills, vomiting) and epigastric abdominal pain for 7 days.  
- Laboratory: Elevated TLC (18,500 cells/ÂµL), neutrophil predominance (80%), mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase (200 IU/L).  
- Ultrasound: Hepatomegaly, large left lobe liver abscess (~350 cc) with signs of impending rupture; mild intrahepatic biliary dilation; intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (~2 mm) between middle and right hepatic vein branches.  
- Doppler: Echogenic content with absent flow at porto-splenic confluence (suggesting thrombosis), collateral formation at porta hepatis indicative of cavernous transformation.  
- CECT: Confirms abscess, porto-splenic vein thrombosis, pinpoint IVC narrowing with small thrombus segment.  
- MRI/MRCP: Pancreatic parenchymal varices in head and body of pancreas draining into splenic vein; prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in left liver lobe; non-visualization of biliary channels distal to CHD = biliary obstruction.  
Clinical picture: Infection (liver abscess) complicated by pylephlebitis (suppurative thrombosis of portal vein system), cavernous transformation of portal vein, portal hypertension, and pancreatic parenchymal varices.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
- Pros: Portal hypertension and varices present; intrahepatic collaterals.  
- Cons: Acute infective presentation (liver abscess), no chronic liver disease history, no imaging signs of cirrhosis (nodular liver surface, atrophy, or typical signs), and presence of acute thrombus due to infection points away from chronic cirrhosis cause.  

2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
- Pros: Intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals could be confused with AVMs.  
- Cons: No typical imaging features of AVM (early venous filling, enlarged feeding arteries), no pulsatile flow on Doppler described, no supporting clinical history.  

3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
- Pros: Classic presentation of pylephlebitis associated with liver abscess: thrombus in portal vein confluence, cavernous transformation, presence of pancreatic varices draining into splenic vein, biliary obstruction, elevated inflammatory markers. Thrombosis secondary to infection fits.  
- Cons: None; matches precisely.  

4) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
- Pros: None relevant; there is no mention of pancreatic cystic lesions or dilatation.  
- Cons: Pancreas shows varices, not cysts; no cystic lesion described.  

5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms  
- Pros: None; no mention of mucinous cystic neoplasm imaging features.  
- Cons: No cystic dilatation of pancreatic duct, no mucinous production or typical imaging signs.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess – matches all clinical and imaging findings exactly.  
2) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation – plausible but lacks chronicity and supporting imaging/lab features.  
3) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM) – less likely, no supportive Doppler or vascular imaging evidence.  
4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms – no imaging or clinical support.  
5) Cystic lesions of pancreas – not supported by imaging.  

<final>Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 31-year-old male with scrotal mass in right hemiscrotum, no relevant past history.  
- Extremely high alpha-fetoprotein (>53,000 µg/L), which is a tumor marker classically associated with yolk sac tumors and some non-seminomatous germ cell tumors.  
- Ultrasound shows a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularization.  
- No normal testicular or epididymal parenchyma visualized on the right side, indicating a large infiltrative mass likely replacing normal tissue.  
- Normal left testis and epididymis.  
- CT shows no metastasis or lymphadenopathy, indicating localized disease at this stage.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1â2 pros/cons each:  
- Mixed germinal cell tumour:  
  Pros: Can present with elevated AFP; heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components fits; can replace testis entirely.  
  Cons: Typically involves both seminomatous and non-seminomatous elements but pure yolk sac markers and presentation may lean more towards specific subtypes.  

- Pure yolk sac tumour:  
  Pros: Extremely high AFP is hallmark; young adult; scrotal mass replacing testis is classic; no metastasis here.  
  Cons: Yolk sac tumors are rare in pure form in adults but possible.  

- Seminoma:  
  Pros: Common testicular tumor in this age group.  
  Cons: Seminomas typically do not produce AFP, especially not extremely high levels; solid hypoechoic mass but usually homogeneous; AFP negative is typical.  

- Choriocarcinoma:  
  Pros: Can produce beta-hCG, not AFP.  
  Cons: Does not produce AFP; very rare and usually very aggressive with early metastasis; no mention of hCG.  

- Teratoma:  
  Pros: Can be heterogeneous with cystic areas.  
  Cons: Usually do not produce very high AFP; more common in children or as part of mixed GCT; no mention of classic fat or calcifications; AFP less characteristic.  

- Lymphoma:  
  Pros: Can present as testicular mass in adults.  
  Cons: No AFP elevation; typically homogeneous; less likely with high AFP.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pure yolk sac tumour: Very high AFP is pathognomonic; the patient's age and presentation fit well; no metastasis yet.  
2) Mixed germinal cell tumour: Also plausible given heterogeneous mass and high AFP, but pure yolk sac less overlap needed for a mixed tumor.  
3) Other candidates less likely due to AFP levels and imaging features.  

Therefore, the best fit is Pure yolk sac tumour.  
</analysis>

<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left
 
 hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI
 
 revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d). Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted (Figure 1d).
 
 CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left (Figure 2).
 
 CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid
 
 internal carotid arteries (
 
 ICAs) (Figure 3). The left
 
 middle
 
 cerebral artery (
 
 MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left
 
 anterior cerebral artery (
 
 ACA) was perfused through the
 
 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (
 
 PCA) was identified.
 
 Digital subtraction angiography (
 
 DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance (Figures 4a and 4b) and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale. Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted. CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left. CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries (ICAs). The left middle cerebral artery (MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left anterior cerebral artery (ACA) was perfused through the anterior communicating (ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA) was identified. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events, Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement, Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia, ICA dissection causing ischemia, Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 39-year-old male with acute left hemiparesis and mild speech slurring.  
- MRI: Acute watershed infarcts in right centrum semiovale. Ivy sign (sulcal hyperintensity) in right temporal region.  
- CT: Hypoplastic left carotid canal.  
- CTA and DSA: Left ICA hypoplasia, bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenosis, altered collateral flow: left MCA perfused via posterior circulation, left ACA via ACom artery, fetal origin left PCA. No "puff of smoke" collateral network observed.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events  
   - Pros: Bilateral supraclinoid ICA stenosis could be atherosclerotic; acute ischemic strokes present.  
   - Cons: ICA hypoplasia and congenital carotid canal hypoplasia less consistent with atherosclerosis. Ivy sign and watershed infarcts more typical of chronic hypoperfusion rather than focal atherosclerosis. No "puff of smoke" suggests non-moyamoya steno-occlusive disease. Patient relatively young for severe atherosclerosis unless high risk (not noted).  

2) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement  
   - Pros: Bilateral ICA stenosis, presence of collateral circulation via ACom and posterior circulation; watershed infarcts indicate chronic hypoperfusion.  
   - Cons: Absence of "puff of smoke" appearance (classic moyamoya collateral network) on angiography argues against classic moyamoya disease/syndrome. Also, ICA hypoplasia and hypoplastic carotid canal are not typical for primary moyamoya, which is usually a progressive idiopathic steno-occlusive disease without congenital large vessel hypoplasia.  

3) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia  
   - Pros: Bilateral ICA stenosis and hypoplasia, compensatory collateral flow via posterior circulation and ACom. Moyamoya disease can sometimes involve congenital or acquired ICA hypoplasia. Ivy sign and watershed infarcts seen in chronic hypoperfusion in moyamoya.  
   - Cons: Again, absence of "puff of smoke" collateral pattern on DSA is atypical; the diagnosis of moyamoya disease usually requires angiographic evidence of characteristic collateral network. However, hypoplastic carotid canal suggests congenital hypoplasia rather than progressive moyamoya pathology, but moyamoya can co-exist.  

4) ICA dissection causing ischemia  
   - Pros: ICA stenosis can cause ischemia.  
   - Cons: Dissections usually acute, unilateral, with vessel wall abnormalities on imaging. No suggestion of dissection on CTA/DSA. Bilateral stenosis and congenital hypoplastic left ICA contradict dissection.  

5) Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries  
   - Pros: Can cause bilateral ICA stenosis and ischemic strokes.  
   - Cons: No clinical or imaging signs of inflammation, vessel wall changes, or systemic vasculitis. Young patient without systemic symptoms. Ivy sign and congenital hypoplastic carotid canal do not fit vasculitis etiology.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia (best fits bilateral stenosis, compensatory collaterals, chronic hypoperfusion signs, though lacking classic "puff of smoke", but presence of bilateral hypoplasia and compensatory flow pathways typical for moyamoya variants).  
2) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement (less likely due to absence of typical collaterals and presence of congenital hypoplasia).  
3) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events (possible but less likely due to age, congenital hypoplasia, and ivy sign).  
4) Vasculitis-induced stenosis (less likely due to lack of inflammatory signs).  
5) ICA dissection (least likely).  

Conclusion: The most consistent diagnosis is Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia.  
</analysis>  
<final> Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia </final><|return|>

   - Pros: ICA stenosis can cause ischemia.  
   - Cons: Dissections usually acute, unilateral, with vessel wall abnormalities on imaging. No suggestion of dissection on CTA/DSA. Bilateral stenosis and congenital hypoplastic left ICA contradict dissection.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia (best fits bilateral stenosis, compensatory collaterals, chronic hypoperfusion signs, though lacking classic "puff of smoke", but presence of bilateral hypoplasia and compensatory flow pathways typical for moyamoya variants).  
2) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement (less likely due to absence of typical collaterals and presence of congenital hypoplasia).  
3) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events (possible but less likely due to age, congenital hypoplasia, and ivy sign).

Conclusion: The most consistent diagnosis is Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 51-year-old female patient, a farmer, presented with two years history of abdominal pain, vomits, diarrhoea, asthenia, weight loss (12%), and arthralgias. Laboratory findings showed
 
 iron deficiency anaemia
 
 , increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, C-reactive protein 6 mg/dL
 
 (normal value < 0.5 mg/dL)
 
 and hypoalbuminemia 2.5 g/dL.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystadenocarcinoma of the right ovary with
 
 suspicion of lymph node metastases
 
 and the patient was referred to our oncology institute.
 
 The exams were re-assessed and the malignant ovarian tumour was excluded, as the MRI was highly suggestive of cystadenofibroma
 
 (Figure 1).
 
 In addition, the CT demonstrated low-attenuation lymph nodes (fatty infiltration) in the left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum and mesentery
 
 (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 3a, and 3b). Furthermore, prominent jejunal folds were seen on T2-weighted images (Figure 4).

 Imaging Findings: Abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystadenocarcinoma of the right ovary with suspicion of lymph node metastases and the patient was referred to our oncology institute. The exams were re-assessed and the malignant ovarian tumour was excluded, as the MRI was highly suggestive of cystadenofibroma. In addition, the CT demonstrated low-attenuation lymph nodes (fatty infiltration) in the left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum and mesentery. Furthermore, prominent jejunal folds were seen on T2-weighted images.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Whipple’s disease, Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease, Lymphoma, Malignant lymph nodes

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 51-year-old female with long-standing abdominal symptoms (pain, vomiting, diarrhea, asthenia, weight loss, arthralgias)  
- Laboratory: iron deficiency anemia, elevated ESR and CRP, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, hypoalbuminemia  
- Imaging: Initial CT and MRI suggested right ovarian cystadenocarcinoma with possible lymph node metastases  
- Reassessment: MRI more suggestive of right ovarian cystadenofibroma (benign)  
- CT showed low-attenuation lymph nodes with fatty infiltration (hypodense), located in left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum, and mesentery  
- MRI also showed prominent jejunal folds on T2-weighted images  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Whipple’s disease  
   - Pros: Can present with weight loss, diarrhea, malabsorption (hypoalbuminemia), arthralgias; mesenteric lymphadenopathy sometimes reported.  
   - Cons: Rarely causes large low-attenuation lymph nodes with fat infiltration; no specific mesenteric fat necrosis or lymph node characteristics like described; typically involves duodenum and proximal jejunum, not prominent jejunal folds; ovarian mass unlikely related.  

2) Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease  
   - Pros: Classic for celiac disease; characterized by low-density mesenteric lymph nodes with fat infiltration; prominent jejunal folds = villous atrophy and mucosal changes typical of celiac disease; systemic symptoms (weight loss, anemia, high inflammatory markers) align well; mesenteric and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy with fat necrosis typical.  
   - Cons: Presence of ovarian cystadenofibroma is incidental but not conflicting; other lymph node sites (supraclavicular, mediastinum) less commonly emphasized but can be involved in systemic inflammation/performance.  

3) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can cause lymphadenopathy (mesenteric, retroperitoneal, mediastinal), constitutional symptoms; abdominal masses possible.  
   - Cons: Lymphoma nodes usually not low attenuation with fatty infiltration on CT; cystadenofibroma would not be confused with lymphoma; mesenteric fat infiltration less typical; lab pattern (hypoalbuminemia, iron deficiency anemia) less typical; no mention of typical lymphoma imaging features.  

4) Malignant lymph nodes (metastases)  
   - Pros: Initially suspected based on imaging; ovarian mass initially thought malignant; lymphadenopathy in multiple sites.  
   - Cons: MRI changed diagnosis to cystadenofibroma (benign); low-attenuation fatty infiltrated nodes less characteristic of metastases; lab and clinical data more systemic inflammatory than metastatic spread; no primary obvious malignant tumor.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease (fits imaging, clinical, lab best)  
2) Whippleâs disease (similar systemic symptoms but lymph node imaging less characteristic)  
3) Lymphoma (lymphadenopathy but imaging and labs less typical)  
4) Malignant lymph nodes (ruled out by imaging reassessment and clinical context)  

Conclusion: Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease best explains low-attenuation fatty lymph nodes, prominent jejunal folds (villous atrophy), and systemic symptoms with laboratory evidence of chronic inflammation and malabsorption. This diagnosis also aligns with known entity in radiology and pathology literature.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

   - Pros: Can appear as a cystic mass in presacral region; often asymptomatic.  
   - Cons: Does not contain fat; not in ischiorectal fossa; no enhancing septae; location wrong; fat attenuation inconsistent.  
   - Pass: No

3) Tailgut cyst (location and fat attenuation misaligned)  
4) Dermoid cyst (location atypical and lack of calcifications)  
5) Perineal epidermal cyst (fat attenuation argue against)  
6) Anterior sacral meningocele (location and fat attenuation incompatible)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.
PostDescription:

   Pros:
   - Classic features include vertebral segmentation defects and multiple costal segmentation defects.

Therefore, the best fit is spondylothoracic dysostosis (LavyâMoseley syndrome).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

- Low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, foramen magnum fullness, possible aqueductal stenosis  
- Ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic, one pelvic)  
- Midgut malrotation  
- Sacral agenesis  
- Rib synostosis  
- Pubic symphysis diastasis  
- Ambiguous genitalia  
- Musculoskeletal abnormalities  
These findings involve multiple systems: abdominal wall defect (omphalocele), genitourinary abnormalities (bladder exstrophy, ectopic kidneys, ambiguous genitalia), anorectal malformation (imperforate anus), spinal abnormalities with dysraphism, craniofacial/brain anomalies, rib and musculoskeletal issues, and sacral agenesis. This constellation is characteristic of the OEIS complex (Omphalocele, Exstrophy, Imperforate anus, Spinal dysraphism) with additional anomalies frequently associated.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) VACTERL association  
   - Pros: Includes vertebral anomalies, anorectal malformations, and other defects (cardiac, tracheoesophageal, renal, limb). Some overlapping features (spinal anomalies, imperforate anus, renal anomalies).  
   - Cons: Does not cover omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, dorsal spinal dysraphism, or cranial anomalies. Also no mention of exstrophy or omphalocele as a defining feature.  

2) Caudal regression syndrome  
   - Pros: Involves sacral and lower vertebral agenesis, lower limb anomalies.  
   - Cons: Does not typically include omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, imperforate anus, or the full set of described anomalies. Omphalocele and bladder exstrophy are not characteristic.  

3) Pentalogy of Cantrell  
   - Pros: Includes abdominal wall defects, including omphalocele.  
   - Cons: Usually consists of five defects: midline supraumbilical abdominal wall defect, ectopia cordis, lower sternal defect, anterior diaphragmatic defect, pericardial defect. Does not include bladder exstrophy, imperforate anus, spinal dysraphism, or the range of anomalies described here.  

4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome  
   - Pros: Associated with macroglossia, omphalocele, organomegaly.  
   - Cons: Typically does not include bladder exstrophy, imperforate anus, spinal dysraphism, or musculoskeletal abnormalities like rib synostosis and spine fusion defects. Not a match for the extensive spinal and GU anomalies.  

5) OEIS complex  
   - Pros: EXACT acronym matches key findings: Omphalocele, Exstrophy of the cloaca (bladder exstrophy), Imperforate anus, Spinal dysraphism (cystic mass with dystrophic neural tissue and vertebral fusion defects). Also, ambiguous genitalia and many of the described associated anomalies are frequently seen.  
   - Cons: Few, but some anomalies like rib synostosis and cranial findings are additional but can coexist.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) OEIS complex (perfect match for the core defining features and many associated anomalies)  
2) VACTERL association (overlaps but missing key exstrophy and omphalocele features)  
3) Caudal regression syndrome (matches sacral agenesis but misses many critical defects)  
4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (omphalocele present but lacks exstrophy and spinal dysraphism)  
5) Pentalogy of Cantrell (abdominal wall defect present but many features absent)  

<final>OEIS (omphalocele, cloacal exstrophy, imperforate anus, and spinal dysraphism) complex</final><|return|>
options: VACTERL association | Caudal regression syndrome | Pentalogy of Cantrell | Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome | OEIS (omphalocele | cloacal exstrophy | imperforate anus | and spinal dysraphism) complex

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp (Figure 1).
 
 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp. Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing a “filarial dance” sign.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dirofilariasis, Epidermal inclusion cyst, Cysticercosis, Foreign body granuloma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 71-year-old female with swelling and itching over left temporal scalp region for 6 months.  
- CT brain: no trauma or intracranial abnormality.  
- CT reveals thick-walled cystic lesion in subcutaneous plane of left temporal scalp.  
- Ultrasound shows cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls, producing a "filarial dance" sign.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Dirofilariasis:  
   Pros: Causes cystic lesions with mobile tubular structures in soft tissues; "filarial dance" sign described in filarial infections.  
   Cons: Dirofilaria is usually a zoonotic infection from dogs and cats, less common in humans; typically involves subcutaneous or muscular tissue. No mention of eosinophilia or travel history, but still plausible.  
2) Epidermal inclusion cyst:  
   Pros: Common subcutaneous cystic lesions on scalp; usually well-defined, may cause swelling.  
   Cons: No internal mobile tubular structures; ultrasound does not show "filarial dance" sign; typically keratinaceous content, not cystic fluid with mobile echogenic structures.  
3) Cysticercosis:  
   Pros: Causes cystic lesions in soft tissues and scalp; cyst contains scolex which can move.  
   Cons: The ultrasound features of actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures is more characteristic of filarial infection; cysticercosis usually shows a cyst with a distinct echogenic scolex, not tubular structures with echogenic walls.  
4) Foreign body granuloma:  
   Pros: Can cause cystic lesions with reaction; usually history of trauma or foreign body.  
   Cons: No trauma history relevant here; no mobile serpiginous structures on ultrasound; ultrasound findings not consistent.  

C) Ranking (most plausible first):  
1) Dirofilariasis: best fits with cystic lesion plus mobile tubular structures on US ("filarial dance" sign).  
2) Cysticercosis: cystic lesion with possible scolex, but US findings less characteristic.  
3) Epidermal inclusion cyst: cystic lesion without internal moving structures.  
4) Foreign body granuloma: less likely given lack of trauma and US findings.  

Thus, Dirofilariasis best matches the described imaging findings (thick-walled cystic lesion with actively mobile, tubular echogenic structures showing "filarial dance" sign).  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Dirofilariasis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An
 
 orthopantomogram
 
 showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion (Figure 1). A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a
 
 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An orthopantomogram showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion. A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus, compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis. During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table, also consistent with osteomas.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cemento-osseous dysplasia, Osteomatosis, Odontomas, Gardner’s syndrome, Hypercementosis, Hereditary multiple exostoses, Fibrous dysplasia
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young female patient (18 years old) with persistent primary teeth.  
- Large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandibular ramus resembling an ivory osteoma.  
- Multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed within the mandible, some with cortical expansion.  
- Multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between tooth roots (hypercementosis).  
- Presence of palpable mass on forehead; CT reveals multiple small sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer skull table, consistent with osteomas.  
- No other symptoms reported; findings include osteomas of craniofacial bones and hypercementosis.

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  
1) Cemento-osseous dysplasia  
   - Pros: May involve jaw bones and cause sclerotic lesions.  
   - Cons: Typically does not cause multiple osteomas of the skull, nor hypercementosis; usually seen in middle-aged African-American women; lesions usually confined to mandibular premolar/molar area, not large exophytic osteomas or skull lesions.

2) Osteomatosis (Polyostotic osteoma)  
   - Pros: Multiple osteomas arising from multiple bones (mandibular ramus and skull outer table) fit well.  
   - Cons: Osteomatosis is rare and not typically associated with persistent primary teeth or hypercementosis.

3) Odontomas  
   - Pros: Can cause persistent primary teeth; odontomas are radiopaque odontogenic tumors.  
   - Cons: Odontomas do not cause extracranial osteomas or hypercementosis; lesion morphology and distribution do not match classic odontoma appearance (mixed radiolucent-radiopaque mass with tooth-like structures).

4) Gardner’s syndrome  
   - Pros: Classic features include multiple osteomas of the skull and jaw, hypercementosis, persistent erupting teeth, and gastrointestinal polyps. The presence of multiple osteomas in the skull and jaw, hypercementosis, and persistent primary teeth strongly support this diagnosis.  
   - Cons: No information on gastrointestinal polyps or other systemic features yet; however, imaging findings are pathognomonic.

5) Hypercementosis  
   - Pros: Radiographic hypercementosis seen.  
   - Cons: Does not explain multiple osteomas or extracranial sclerotic lesions in skull or mandibular ramus exophytic osteoma.

6) Hereditary multiple exostoses (HMEs)  
   - Pros: Multiple bone growths/exostoses.  
   - Cons: HMEs mainly involve long bones, not craniofacial bones; lesions are cartilage-capped exostoses rather than ivory osteomas.

7) Fibrous dysplasia  
   - Pros: Can cause bone expansion and sclerotic lesions in jaw.  
   - Cons: Usually has a "ground glass" matrix and not multiple mature sclerotic osteomas; would not produce extracranial osteomas or hypercementosis.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Gardner’s syndrome: Pathognomonic combination of multiple craniofacial osteomas, hypercementosis, and persistent primary teeth fits perfectly.  
2) Osteomatosis: Reasonable but lacks hypercementosis and typical clinical context.  
3) Cemento-osseous dysplasia: Partial fit, but lacks osteomas and hypercementosis.  
4) Odontomas: Explains persistent teeth but not osteomas.  
5) Hereditary multiple exostoses: Site mismatch.  
6) Fibrous dysplasia: Matrix mismatch and absence of osteomas.  
7) Hypercementosis alone: Incomplete explanation.

<final>Gardner’s syndrome</final><|return|>
options: Cemento-osseous dysplasia | Osteomatosis | Odontomas | Gardner’s syndrome | Hypercementosis | Hereditary multiple exostoses | Fibrous dysplasia

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm
 
 (ap/axial/cc) (
 
 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (
 
 small arrow in Figure 2
 
 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-
 
 enhancing
 
 fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe (arrows on Figure 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm. Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level. Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-enhancing fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Retropharyngeal abscess, Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst, Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy, Suppurative thyroiditis
FinalDiagnosis: Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 9-year-old female with fever and neck movement restriction  
- Posterior bulging of left-sided oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal mucosa, but no pyriform sinus irregularities  
- Contrast-enhanced CT shows a well-defined 2x2x1 cm rim-enhancing fluid collection in the retropharyngeal space with surrounding oedema  
- Presence of a thin rim-enhancing fistulous tract connecting the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Retropharyngeal abscess  
   - Pros: Rim-enhancing fluid collection in the retropharyngeal space with edema is classic for abscess; presents with fever and neck pain/movement restrictions.  
   - Cons: The fistulous tract connecting to the thyroid gland is unusual for a simple abscess and suggests an underlying source of infection tracking to the thyroid.  

2) Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst  
   - Pros: Branchial cleft cysts can become infected and show rim enhancement. Fistulous tracts may be present.  
   - Cons: Typical location of third/fourth branchial cleft cysts is near carotid or hypopharyngeal spaces, not primarily retropharyngeal space; no mention of cyst architecture typical of branchial remnants. Also, fistula usually follows path to piriform sinus, here the fistula is retro- and connects to thyroid pole, not typical.  

3) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy  
   - Pros: Retropharyngeal lymph nodes can undergo necrosis and develop rim enhancing masses.  
   - Cons: Presence of a fistulous tract to the thyroid is not typical for adenopathy; usually confined to nodal disease without fistula tract.  

4) Suppurative thyroiditis  
   - Pros: Fistulous tract connecting from retropharyngeal abscess to upper pole of left thyroid suggests infection extending from retropharyngeal space to thyroid lobe. Fever and neck pain are consistent.  
   - Cons: Primary thyroiditis would normally present with thyroid enlargement and inflammation; here primary lesion is retropharyngeal abscess with secondary extension to thyroid via fistula, not primary thyroiditis. However, suppurative thyroiditis can be secondary to such fistula.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Retropharyngeal abscess (primary lesion fits classic presentation)  
2) Suppurative thyroiditis (secondary involvement via fistula, but primary abscess is retropharyngeal)  
3) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy (less likely given fistula)  
4) Atypically located infected branchial cleft cyst (less consistent anatomic site and findings)  

Given the primary lesion is the well-defined rim-enhancing retropharyngeal fluid collection with a fistula tract to the thyroid, the best diagnosis is a retropharyngeal abscess with secondary involvement of the thyroid gland.

</analysis><final>Retropharyngeal abscess</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.
PostDescription:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 2-day-old male with pyloric atresia and left thigh cutaneous lesion.
- Multiple hypoechoic liver lesions identified on ultrasound, with lesion in segment IVb growing from 6 mm to 34 mm over a few days.
- On liver MRI, lesion 1 is large (34 mm), T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative rectangular shape with capsular retraction.
- Lesions 2 and 3 are smaller, T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity.
- None of the lesions show enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.
- Clinical context includes surgery and transient jaundice.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Hepatoblastoma
   - Pros: Common liver tumor in infants; can be hypoechoic and large; may present with elevated AFP (not mentioned).
   - Cons: Typically shows arterial enhancement and washout; diffusion restriction often present; capsular retraction can occur but enhancement is usually seen; no mention of AFP elevation; multiple small lesions less common; lack of enhancement and diffusion restriction argue against.
2) Lymphoma
   - Pros: Can involve liver; may present with hypoechoic lesions.
   - Cons: Rare in neonates; usually shows diffusion restriction and sometimes enhancement; no mention of systemic symptoms; multiple large lesions without enhancement or diffusion restriction are atypical.
3) Infantile myofibromatosis
   - Pros: Typically presents in infants with cutaneous lesions; can have visceral involvement including liver; skin lesion in thigh present; lesions can be hypoechoic and T2 hyperintense; capsular retraction and infiltrative growth pattern described in visceral myofibromas; lack of enhancement and diffusion restriction can be seen.
   - Cons: Rare tumor but fits clinical scenario well.
4) Neuroblastoma
   - Pros: Common pediatric tumor; can metastasize to liver; can have hypoechoic liver lesions.
   - Cons: Usually presents with adrenal or paraspinal primary; enhancement usually present; age is compatible but no primary mass noted; lack of enhancement and diffusion restriction argue against.
5) Haemangiomata
   - Pros: Common liver lesion in infants; can be hypoechoic; T2 hyperintense.
   - Cons: Typically show peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in; absence of enhancement makes this unlikely; capsular retraction and infiltrative pattern unusual.

C) Ranking:
1) Infantile myofibromatosis - best fits clinical and imaging findings (cutaneous lesion, multiple hypoechoic liver lesions without enhancement or diffusion restriction, capsular retraction, infiltrative growth).
2) Hepatoblastoma - plausible but lack of enhancement and diffusion restriction less supportive.
3) Lymphoma - unlikely due to age and imaging features.
4) Neuroblastoma - less likely without primary mass or typical imaging features.
5) Haemangiomata - excluded due to lack of characteristic enhancement.

<final> Infantile myofibromatosis </final><|return|>
options: Hepatoblastoma | Lymphoma | Infantile myofibromatosis | Neuroblastoma | Haemangiomata

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman with a known history of retroperitoneal sarcoma, who was receiving radiotherapy, was referred for evaluation of newly palpable lumps in her left breast and left axillary region.
 Imaging Findings: A diagnostic bilateral mammogram (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d; previous mammogram is not available for comparison) shows two circumscribed masses in the left breast upper-outer quadrant.
 
 On the ultrasound of the left breast and axilla (Figures 2a and 2b), there are two adjacent hypo-echoic masses with circumscribed borders in the left breast at 2
 
 o’clock; m
 
 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.
 
 The abdominal CT scan with contrast (Figures 3a and 3b) shows a large heterogeneously enhancing mass in the retroperitoneal space with left kidney invasion and aortic wall encasement.
 
 The histopathologic exam reveals highly atypical spindle cells with large pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an inferior glenohumeral ligament (IGHL) lesion. Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion. However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast. Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation. This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema. Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences, indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function. MRA also identified a superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion, which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligament (HALG injury) with superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion, Bony humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligament (HALG injury), Bony Bankart lesion, Rotator-cuff tear/tendinosis
FinalDiagnosis: Humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligament (HALG injury) with superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres to around 22x15 mm, and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures, with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Hypervascular lung metastases, Pulmonary sequestration, Bronchocoele, Pulmonary varix, Pulmonary arterial aneurysm, Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations, Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 67-year-old female with known malignant renal mass undergoing cancer staging.  
- Contrast-enhanced CT includes thoracic, abdominal, and pelvic regions.  
- Multiple peripheral lung nodules with tissular densities, ranging from a few mm to ~22x15 mm.  
- Lesions enhance during arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures.  
- Identified feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins.  
- Lesions are located peripherally in both lungs, multiple, and vascular with arterial and venous phases.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hypervascular lung metastases  
   - Pros: Patient has a known malignancy; hypervascular lung mets can be present.  
   - Cons: Typically, hypervascular mets do not show the same enhancement pattern as vascular structures, nor do they typically have clear feeding arteries and venous drainage directly visualized. Usually more solid nodules without clear vascular feeding/draining vessels.  
2) Pulmonary sequestration  
   - Pros: Presence of abnormal systemic arterial supply; congenital lesion.  
   - Cons: Usually a single focal lesion, often lobar or segmental, rarely multiple; sequestration has systemic arterial supply (usually aorta), not lobar arterial supply; not typical in multiple small peripheral nodules.  
3) Bronchocoele  
   - Pros: Airfilled dilated bronchi; might appear as tubular opacities.  
   - Cons: Not vascular enhancement; no feeding arteries/draining veins; different imaging characteristics.  
4) Pulmonary varix  
   - Pros: Vascular dilation of pulmonary veins; can appear as vascular lesion.  
   - Cons: Usually localized, single vascular dilation rather than multiple nodules; not associated with feeding arterial vessels or multiple lesions.  
5) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm  
   - Pros: Vascular lesion with arterial enhancement.  
   - Cons: Usually single or few focal aneurysms; less common to have multiple peripheral arterial aneurysms in this pattern; does not explain venous drainage.  
6) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (PAVM)

Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

- Myositis ossificans: Usually follows local trauma, starts as soft tissue mass with peripheral ossification; typically unilateral and more focal; can be confused early on but mature bilateral periarticular ossification less common without bilateral trauma.

- Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva: Rare genetic disease causing progressive heterotopic ossification; usually starts in early childhood and is widespread; patient age and acute precipitating event less typical.  
- Tumoral calcinosis: Typically shows lobular calcified masses around joints, not mature heterotopic bone formation; presentation different.  
- Sarcoma: No mass lesion or destructive features suggesting malignancy; ossific density is mature, bilateral, non-destructive; malignant process unlikely.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification (best match for TBI, immobilization, bilateral mature ossification around joints)  
2) Myositis ossificans (less likely because of bilateral symmetrical mature ossification without direct trauma to shoulders)  
3) Other less likely based on imaging and clinical context.  

Therefore, Neurogenic heterotopic ossification is the correct diagnosis.

Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male infant presented with a palpable mass on his arm. The parents noticed it since he was a month and a half old. Clinical examination showed a painless mass without signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).
