OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydronephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder. Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised. After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis. The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Post-transplant renal artery stent, Post-transplant renal vein stenting, Post-transplant ureteral stent removal, Post-transplant ureteral stent placement

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 63-year-old woman with recurrent foramen magnum meningioma.  
- Preop MRI: extra-axial lesion 3.9 x 2.1 cm causing mass effect on brainstem (bulb) and upper spinal cord, with posterior and left displacement. 
- Postop day 2: partial resection, persistent tumor, and signal changes in brainstem and cervical spinal cord: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, T1 hypointensity.  
- Diffusion imaging shows mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema, consistent with ischemic-edematous changes.  
- Clinically, immediate postoperative tetraplegia with preserved consciousness and need for ventilation.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Central cord syndrome: Usually involves cervical spinal cord trauma with motor and sensory deficits greater in upper limbs than lower limbs, often due to hyperextension injury or disc herniation. In this case, the lesion is at the foramen magnum, compressing brainstem and upper spinal cord, and the distribution is tetraplegia (all four limbs). No classic central cord MR signal pattern. Also, central cord syndrome is trauma-related, not postoperative tumor-related ischemia.  
2) White cord syndrome: A rare postoperative complication of decompressive surgery for cervical myelopathy, characterized by paradoxical worsening of neurological status despite adequate decompression, due to reperfusion injury causing white matter swelling (hyperintensity on T2), vasogenic-cytotoxic edema. Here, postoperative ischemic-edematous changes and immediate tetraplegia after decompression are consistent. The location (bulb and upper cord) is consistent with the surgical site (foramen magnum).  
3) Compressive myelopathy: This is preoperative scenario, as imaging shows preoperative compression. However, postoperative imaging no longer shows new direct compression but signal changes consistent with ischemia/edema. Clinically worsen after surgery, not before. Hence, postoperative changes are not due to new compression.  
4) Epidural hematoma: Typically shows a biconvex or lentiform mass causing mass effect postoperatively, hyperintense on T1 (subacute) or variable signal, compressing spinal cord. No mention of hemorrhagic mass or typical imaging features; also signal alterations are described as edema, not hematoma. Moreover, the patient had immediate neurological decline without mention of new hematoma on MRI.  

C) Ranking:  
1) White cord syndrome - matches postoperative ischemic-edematous changes, immediate tetraplegia after adequate decompression, consistent with reperfusion injury.  
2) Central cord syndrome - less likely due to typical trauma etiology and clinical features not matching.  
3) Epidural hematoma - ruled out by lack of imaging evidence.  
4) Compressive myelopathy - preoperative finding, not explanatory for postoperative decline.  

Tie-break: White cord syndrome is pathognomonic for postoperative reperfusion injury with ischemic changes and clinical worsening despite decompression - exactly the scenario described.  

</analysis>  
<final>White cord syndrome</final><|return|>

 spine

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old female with a known history of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) presented with atraumatic pain and weakness of the left leg. She had previously had a focal neurofibroma excised from the left thigh. MRI imaging of the left femur and knee was performed to identify a cause for the pain.
 Imaging Findings: MRI scan of the left knee (Figures 1, 2, and 3) demonstrated diffuse T2 bright and iso-hypotense T1 tissue infiltrating the subcutaneous and intermuscular planes at the posterior aspect of the knee and reaching the periosteal surface of the distal femur, causing bone dysplasia with widening of the posterior intercondylar notch. CT imaging also demonstrated the marked widening of the intercondylar notch (Figures 4 and 5).

Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 10-year history of limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis presented with insidious pain and swelling of the left thumb’s fingertip. Physical examination revealed sclerodactyly and a tender nodule on the distal phalanx of the first finger.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographic imaging of the left hand (Figures 1a and 1b) and thumb (Figure 2) revealed a well-defined calcification in the soft tissue overlying the distal phalanx of the left first finger, measuring approximately 1.5 cm. Periarticular osteopenia was also observed, particularly evident at the thumb (Figure 1b). Cortical thinning and blurring of the margins were noted in the proximal interphalangeal joint of the ring finger (Figure 1a). No other significant findings were identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 58-year-old male with worsening nausea for the past 72 hours presented to the emergency department with severe chest pain radiating to the back in the context of 4 recent episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis. He was afebrile, diaphoretic, and mildly tachycardic. Cardiac workup, complete blood count, and electrolytes were normal.
 Imaging Findings: A chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced chest CT were performed. The X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum extending into the neck and a tiny left apical pneumothorax. Notably, left pleural effusion with atelectasis and consolidation were also seen (Figure 1a). The coronal CT images were closely correlated to the X-ray findings, clearly showing the pneumomediastinum extending superiorly towards the neck soft tissues. A pneumothorax at the left lower lung base, not previously seen on X-ray, is also demonstrated on coronal CT view (Figure 1b).
 
 The axial chest CT image on lung window showed evidence of pneumomediastinum with air around the oesophagus. Left pneumothorax and bilateral pleural effusions are also seen (Figure 2). On the soft tissue window, a thickened distal oesophagus was seen; however, no clear site of perforation was appreciated. Bilateral complex attenuating pleural effusions were also observed but there was no obvious contrast extravasation (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1 is a 54-year-old female who experienced right ankle pain after an injury, leading to the clinical suspicion of Achilles tendon rupture, and was evaluated by radiograph and MRI after 1 week.
 
 Case 2 is a 62-year-old male who reported calf pain, raising concerns for deep vein thrombosis, further evaluated with ultrasound. He noted a trivial trauma
 
 occurred 10 days prior.
 Imaging Findings: In Case 1, the lateral ankle radiograph revealed increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcification (Figure 1). MRI indicated significant disruption (95%) of the distal 5.5 cm of the tendon, cystic areas, and signs of haemorrhage (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4). Extensive oedema in the posterior lower leg muscles led to a screening of the leg that identified a 2.8 x 1.6 x 5.4 cm collection in the intermuscular fascial plane, causing indentation on the soleus. The plantaris tendon was partially torn, while the soleus and medial gastrocnemius exhibited grade 1 strain without fibre discontinuity (Figures 5a and 5b).
 
 In Case 2, the ultrasound showed a bulky distal 6.0 cm Achilles tendon with altered echotexture, multiple calcific foci, and a high-grade partial thickness tear (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c). An anechoic collection measuring 3.3 x 1.2 x 8.6 cm was observed in the intermuscular fascial plane, between the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, with no increased vascularity noted on the Doppler study (Figures 7a and 7b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male patient on anticoagulation therapy, with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency, underwent a standard chest X-ray as part of the preoperative evaluation for a total hip replacement. He was asymptomatic on the respiratory level, with no dyspnoea or chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: The initial X-ray revealed a likely pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan was then performed, demonstrating an extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm (AP × TR × CC). The collection was spontaneously hyperdense, suggesting the presence of blood or fluid with a high protein content. Additionally, the “extra-pleural fat sign” was observed, confirming an extra-pleural process rather than a pleural or lung lesion (Figures 2 and 3).
 
 In the presence of renal insufficiency and an extra-pleural mass, contrast injection was not performed. Consequently, a mass with potential bleeding could not be definitively ruled out, and an MRI was conducted. T1-weighted MRI images showed the extra-pleural collection appearing hyperintense due to the presence of blood products (Figure 4). In T2-weighted images, the lesion was mildly hyperintense, consistent with the presence of subacute haemorrhage or an organised haematoma (Figure 5).
 
 On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), a hyperintense signal with ADC restriction was observed (Figures 6a and 6b). After gadolinium injection, a peripheral enhancement of the lesion’s capsule was observed, with no enhancement observed within the lesion itself (Figure 7).
 
 This supports the primary haemorrhagic nature of the lesion rather than an inflammatory or neoplastic process.
 
 Non-contrast CT and MRI scans of the thorax were performed using T1, T2, DWI, and T1 fat-saturated gadolinium sequences in a Philips Ingenia 1.5T high-field MRI scanner and a Philips Incisive 32-slice CT scanner.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 81-year-old male on anticoagulation therapy with acute renal insufficiency.  
- Chest X-ray shows right peripheral pleural opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (suggesting extra-pleural nature).  
- CT shows extra-pleural collection measuring 30 x 72 x 87 mm, spontaneously hyperdense (consistent with blood or proteinaceous fluid).  
- "Extra-pleural fat sign" present (confirming extra-pleural location).  
- No gadolinium contrast used on CT due to renal insufficiency.  
- MRI T1: hyperintense lesion (blood products).  
- MRI T2: mildly hyperintense (subacute haemorrhage or organized haematoma).  
- DWI: hyperintense signal with ADC restriction.  
- Post-gadolinium T1 fat-saturated MRI: peripheral rim enhancement of capsule, no internal enhancement.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  
1) Encysted pleural effusion  
- Pros: pleural fluid, peripheral location.  
- Cons: typically not hyperdense spontaneously; no extra-pleural fat sign; typically internal fluid signal not hyperdense or showing rim enhancement.  
2) Empyema  
- Pros: pleural fluid with rim enhancement.  
- Cons: usually ill-defined margins, often acute angles with lung, more complex fluid with air-fluid levels; hyperdense on CT can occur but less likely; no clinical infection signs; extra-pleural fat sign not usually described.  
3) Pleural malignant neoplasm  
- Pros: rim enhancement possible; solid component usually enhancing.  
- Cons: no internal enhancement; hyperdense spontaneous density less typical; blood products on T1/T2 not typical; extra-pleural fat sign less relevant (neoplasms usually invade pleura) and no clinical signs.  
4) Extra-pleural haematoma  
- Pros: hyperdense spontaneous lesion on CT; T1 hyperintensity due to blood products; peripheral rim enhancement (capsule); extra-pleural fat sign present; patient on anticoagulation and acute renal insufficiency (risk factors); DWI restriction consistent with recent hematoma.  
- Cons: none prominent; fits well with timeline and imaging.  
5) Parapneumonic effusion  
- Pros: pleural effusion adjacent to pneumonia.  
- Cons: no infection signs; no acute angle; no lung parenchymal disease; hyperdensity less typical; no enhancement inside; extra-pleural fat sign absent.  
6) Solitary fibrous tumour  
- Pros: extra-pleural possible; can show peripheral rim enhancement.  
- Cons: usually enhancing solid mass; no T1 hyperintensity from blood; no hyperdense spontaneous blood; no ADC restriction typical for tumor; no patient symptoms.  
7) Intercostal nerve tumour  
- Pros: para-pleural/extra-pleural location.  
- Cons: typically solid enhancing mass; no spontaneous hyperdensity; no blood products; no rim enhancement pattern; no clinical symptoms.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Extra-pleural haematoma: matches clinical scenario (anticoagulation, acute renal failure), imaging findings (hyperdense lesion, extra-pleural fat sign, T1/T2 characteristics of blood), no internal enhancement indicating non-neoplastic nature.  
2) Pleural malignant neoplasm: less likely due to no internal enhancement and imaging suggests blood products.  
3) Empyema: less likely due to no infection signs and imaging features not typical.  
4) Encysted pleural effusion: less likely due to hyperdense and blood product signs.  
5) Solitary fibrous tumour: less likely due to lack of solid enhancing tumor characteristics.  
6) Intercostal nerve tumour: least likely due to imaging and clinical mismatch.  
7) Parapneumonic effusion: least likely due to clinical and imaging inconsistency.  

<final> Extra-pleural haematoma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis presents with an acute onset of significant haemoptysis. He mentions experiencing weight loss, tiredness, and intermittent fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for tuberculosis, which was treated a decade ago. A chest X-ray performed recently demonstrated cavitary abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: CT angiography provided the main findings, consisting of a localised aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch located near a tuberculous cavity (Figures 2a and 2b), representing the aneurysm. Hyperdense opacity with surrounding haematoma indicates active bleeding, while multiple air locules within the opacity are characteristic of a tuberculous cavity (Figures 1a and 1b), distinguishing it from other pulmonary lesions. Centrilobular ground-glass nodules may be observed around the haematoma, suggesting endobronchial dissemination of blood (Figure 4). Post-contrast imaging reveals focal enhancement of the aneurysm, aiding in precise assessment of its size, shape, and location (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old female was admitted to the emergency department due to a 2-week history of constipation, drooling, and left eye strabismus due to oculomotor nerve paralysis. Further physical examination without clinically significant findings. Initial laboratory tests upon admission showed white blood count (WBC), neutrophils, and lymphocytes within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: An initial contrast-enhanced CT scan of the brain (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed a lesion that extended to cranial structures and temporal lobes, involving deep neck spaces.
 
 An initial contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) showed pleural thickening, subpleural nodules, pancreatic lesions with dilated pancreatic duct calibre, bilateral renal cortical lesions, and a right paraspinal lesion invading the spinal canal at L2–L3 with nerve roots involvement, suggestive of metastasis. Pelvic lymphadenopathies were identified.
 
 A 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) revealed a smaller lesion compared to the previous CT, with decreased intracranial and cerebral extension and a reduced cerebral and cerebellar volume.
 
 The 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) showed no pleural thickening areas or subpleural nodules. The pancreas with a size reduction of the focal lesions. The pancreatic duct calibre was preserved, and the kidneys had no infiltrative lesions. Generalised reduction of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The paraspinal lesion was not visible. A not previously identified generalised fatty infiltration was shown.

3) Primary visceral organ malignancies
   - Pros: Pancreas and kidney lesions could be primary cancers.
   - Cons: Highly unlikely in a 2-year-old; dissemination pattern unusual; no primary organ identified; improvement with systemic therapy less consistent.

4) Sarcoidosis
   - Pros: Multiorgan involvement, lymphadenopathy, pleural thickening.
   - Cons: Extremely rare in this age; no pulmonary lymphadenopathy typically; labs not supportive; lesion regression within 2 months unlikely without steroids; neuro involvement unusual in sarcoidosis at this age.

C) Ranking:
1) Lymphoma (most likely given age, presentation, widespread lesions, good response to therapy)
2) Metastatic pro-B type acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (possible but labs and duct dilation less typical)
3) Primary visceral organ malignancies (unlikely at this age)
4) Sarcoidosis (least likely)

The response favors lymphoma because the clinical and imaging features (multifocal organ involvement, lymphadenopathy, perineural invasion, pleural and nodal disease) and the dramatic improvement with systemic therapy (presumed) are characteristic. The pancreatic duct dilation can be due to mass effect from lymphoma infiltration, not a typical finding but possible.
options: Metastatic pro-B type acute lymphoblastic leukaemia | Lymphoma | Primary visceral organ malignancies | Sarcoidosis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: In an ambulatory appointment, a 29-year-old female patient presented with chronic and persistent cough. No smoking history was referred, and the physical examination was unremarkable. An ambulatory chest x-ray was performed in our institution and further evaluation with computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), was later requested.
 Imaging Findings: The chest x-ray (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed an opacity, with rounded and well-defined borders, localised in the posterior and medial regions of the right middle lung field.
 
 Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT /Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) revealed in the right paravertebral region, next to the posteromedial aspect of the 7
 
 th
 
 and 8
 
 costal arches, a large nodular lesion, with 47 x 49 x 41 mm (L x AP x T), ovoid with regular and well-defined borders, heterogeneous and with marked peripheral contrast uptake. It is localised in an extrapulmonary topography
 
 , as it is centred on the chest wall and shows obtuse angles with the thoracic wall, and it is near proximity to
 
 nerve structures. There were no signs of invasion of adjacent structures, particularly bone structures.
 
 The contrast-enhanced thoracic MRI images (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) confirmed the localisation and nature of the mass, further characterising it as a soft tissue lesion showing heterogeneity of signal in the T2-weighted sequences, with areas of interstitial hyperintensity and markedly heterogeneous uptake after contrast administration.

   + Pros: Location near intercostal nerves and chest wall paravertebral area fits; well-defined borders; heterogenous enhancement possible; typical benign neurogenic tumor; peripheral contrast uptake can be seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient presented to our department for routine follow-up imaging after left radical orchiectomy 19 years earlier due to a testicular germ cell tumour, and with retroperitoneal recurrence 11 years after surgery. He had a previous history of left orchidopexy during childhood to repair cryptorchidism.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, who underwent a left radical orchiectomy for a testicular germ cell tumour, the abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) revealed tubular-shaped splenic tissue along the left paracolic gutter, representing a retroperitoneal splenic cord connecting the spleen to the remaining mesonephric structure in the left lower quadrant (Figures 1a to 1i). Additionally, this condition was associated with congenital absence of lower limbs (Figure 2).
 
 Retrospectively, the described abdominal features were considered stable compared to the oldest examination available in our database, which was performed at the time of the retroperitoneal recurrence (Figures 3a to 3f).

- Congenital absence of lower limbs noted.  
- Retroperitoneal splenic tissue is stable over time.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Splenogonadal fusion  
  Pros: Classic presentation includes ectopic splenic tissue fused or adjacent to gonadal structures; history of cryptorchidism and orchidopexy is relevant as splenogonadal fusion often involves testes or remnants; tubular splenic tissue along left paracolic gutter fits pattern.  
  Cons: None significant; history and imaging findings align well.  
- Splenomegaly  
  Pros: Enlarged spleen could present with adjacent splenic tissue on imaging.  
  Cons: Splenomegaly is diffuse spleen enlargement, not ectopic or tubular splenic tissue in retroperitoneum; no mention of increased spleen size or diffuse changes; congenital absence of lower limbs irrelevant to splenomegaly.  

C) Ranking:  
1. Splenogonadal fusion (best match; tubal splenic tissue along left paracolic gutter in context of cryptorchidism and orchidopexy; classic for this rare congenital anomaly).  
2. Splenomegaly (does not account for ectopic retroperitoneal splenic tissue or history).  
No ties.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old female with poor feeding toleration, aspirating all consistencies of food, with failure to thrive, was planned for gastrostomy tube placement, and an upper gastrointestinal study was requested for the anatomic assessment.
 Imaging Findings: A fluoroscopic upper gastrointestinal study was done by injection of barium via the indwelling enteric tube. The stomach exhibited a normal appearance and was normally emptied through a normal pylorus, duodenal bulb, and descending duodenum (Figure 1). After an initial descent of the duodenum, the third part of the duodenum did not cross the midline as expected. Instead, it ascends on the right side of the spine posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum up to the level of the duodenal bulb and then completes its transverse course and reaches the left upper abdomen (Figures 2 and 3).

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Duodenum inversum:
   - Pros: The described anomalous course of the third part of the duodenum (ascending posteriorly on the right side of the spine) matches classic description of duodenum inversum, where the third portion ascends abnormally.
   - Cons: None significant; findings are typical for this rare anomaly.

2) Redundant duodenum:
   - Pros: Could cause looping and abnormal positions, but usually describes elongated or redundant loops without abnormal directions.
   - Cons: Redundant duodenum does not usually cause the third portion to ascend posteriorly before crossing midline; the path described is more specific than just redundancy.

3) Intestinal malrotation:
   - Pros: Can cause abnormal positioning of duodenum and small bowel.
   - Cons: In malrotation, the duodenum typically does not cross midline properly, but the third portion usually lies to the right of midline rather than ascending posteriorly and then crossing; also, malrotation often presents with a corkscrew sign and can have volvulus signs, which are not described here.

C) Ranking:
1) Duodenum inversum - best fits the described anatomic abnormality (third part ascending posteriorly on right side, parallel to descending duodenum, then crossing).
2) Intestinal malrotation - possible but less specific; no mention of classic malrotation features.
3) Redundant duodenum - less specific and does not explain described abnormal third part course.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old female patient presented with headache, visual disturbances, and bitemporal hemianopia. Hormonal assays indicated abnormal levels of serum prolactin and growth hormone.
 Imaging Findings: The patient’s MRI brain showed a large lobulated sellar lesion (Figure 1) on post-contrast sagittal images, showing suprasellar extension and anteriorly extending into both sphenoid sinuses and ethmoid sinuses. Axial T2 image (Figure 2) shows the lesion to be predominantly involving the left cavernous sinus and encasing the left internal carotid artery, and seen extending into the left orbital apex and left frontal and temporal lobes. FLAIR coronal image (Figure 3) shows compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem. In the post-contrast axial image (Figure 4), the lesion was seen extending into the right cavernous sinus with suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.

- Lesion shows post-contrast enhancement (implied by contrast images)  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Meningioma  
   - Pros: Meningiomas can encase vessels, extend into sinuses, and cause bone hyperostosis. They often enhance strongly and can be lobulated.  
   - Cons: Typically, meningiomas arise from dura, not sellar region. They are less likely to cause hormonal abnormalities like prolactin and GH elevation. Also, usually not directly pituitary in origin or cause bitemporal hemianopia unless very large and compressive.  
2) Pituitary macroadenoma  
   - Pros: Common cause of sellar lesions with suprasellar extension, causing bitemporal hemianopia. Can secrete prolactin and growth hormone, causing hormonal elevation. Typically lobulated and enhance with contrast. Can extend into cavernous sinuses and sphenoid sinuses, encase internal carotid artery, and compress adjacent brain structures.  
   - Cons: Very aggressive and extensive invasion into orbital apex and frontal/temporal lobes is unusual but possible in large macroadenomas.  
3) Chordoma  
   - Pros: Occur in clival region near the skull base, can extend into sphenoid sinus and cause bone destruction.  
   - Cons: Usually midline, midclival lesions, bone destructive, not typically in sellar region or causing hormonal abnormalities. Does not invade cavernous sinus or orbital apex commonly. Does not secrete prolactin or GH.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pituitary macroadenoma â best fits clinical symptoms (bitemporal hemianopia), hormonal findings (prolactin and GH elevation), and imaging features (sellar origin, suprasellar extension, cavernous sinus involvement, bilateral extension, sinusal involvement).  
2) Meningioma â possible but less likely given hormonal abnormalities and primary sellar location.  
3) Chordoma â least likely given lesion location and absence of typical chordoma features (midline clival location, bone destruction, lack of hormonal activity).  

Therefore, the best diagnosis is pituitary macroadenoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Five days after an uncomplicated embolisation of an asymptomatic, growing right middle cerebral artery (MCA) aneurysm, a 56-year-old woman comes to the ER due to headache, a seizure and fluctuant left upper extremity weakness and left campimetric defects. A Todd’s paralysis is suspected, and the patient is admitted for a workup.
 Imaging Findings: Initially, a brain CT is performed, which shows no significant abnormalities apart from the embolisation material (Figure 1).
 
 Then a brain MRI is performed, including the following sequences: DWI (Figures 2a and 2b), SWI (Figure 3), T2, FLAIR (Figure 4), 3D TOF and pre and post-gadolinium T1 (Figures 5a and 5b). The MRI discloses multiple round lesions varying in size in the right hemisphere. The lesions are spontaneously T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense, the largest ones showing a small, central T2 hypointensity. Post-gadolinium enhancement is demonstrated in all the lesions. No restricted diffusion or susceptibility artefacts are present.
 
 Follow-up imaging after 6 months (Figures 6, 7, 8a, and 8b) shows overall improvement with resolution of the enhancing foci. However, T2 hyperintensity is still present, and central punctate susceptibility artefacts have appeared, interpreted as microhaemorrhagic foci.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient status: 5 days post embolisation of a right MCA aneurysm.
- Clinical presentation: seizure, fluctuating neurological deficits (Todd's paralysis suspected).
- Imaging: Initial CT unremarkable except embolisation material. MRI with DWI, SWI, T2, FLAIR, 3D TOF and gadolinium contrast.
- Lesion characteristics: Multiple round lesions in right hemisphere, T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense; largest lesions have small central T2 hypointensity. All enhance post-gadolinium.
- No restricted diffusion or susceptibility artifacts initially.
- Follow-up MRI at 6 months: overall improvement, resolution of enhancement, persistent T2 hyperintensity, new central punctate susceptibility artifacts (microhemorrhages).

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Non-ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions:
   - Pros: Lesions are non-ischemic (no restricted diffusion), multiple enhancing lesions in right hemisphere, post-embolisation setting fits with foreign body/inflammation due to embolisation material. Absence of diffusion restriction supports non-ischemic lesion.
   - Cons: NICE lesions usually include post-procedure inflammatory changes, but microhemorrhages later could be related to this; but absence of other infective signs (no rim enhancement or abscess formation).

2) Subacute cerebral infarcts:
   - Pros: Subacute infarcts may be T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense, and sometimes enhance.
   - Cons: Would typically show restricted diffusion acutely/subacutely, absent here. Also, lesions are multiple round enhancing foci, not typical infarcts; clinical and imaging timeline inconsistent with infarcts. Also, no DWI restriction initially.

3) Cerebral microabscesses:
   - Pros: Can show multiple enhancing lesions.
   - Cons: Would typically have restricted diffusion (central pus) on DWI, absent here. No ring enhancement noted. Clinical context without fever or infection signs; no rim enhancement; no diffusion restriction → microabscess unlikely.

4) Brain metastases:
   - Pros: Multiple enhancing lesions possible; subacute, variable size.
   - Cons: No primary cancer history; lesions appear right hemisphere only (asymmetrical but could be random); metastatic lesions often have more vasogenic edema and ring enhancement; also enhancement resolves on follow-up with residual T2 signal and microhemorrhage (unusual), and initial absence of diffusion restriction does not fit typical metastases at this stage. Patient post-embolisation with no infection or cancer history → metastases unlikely.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old female presented with a four-day history of sudden onset frontal headache, vomiting, ophthalmalgia, photophobia and blurred vision. Her past medical history included migraines, right-sided phacoemulsion and an intraocular lens implant six years ago. Fundoscopy demonstrated choroidal folds and effusions. Laboratory blood workup revealed only elevated serum ACE levels.
 Imaging Findings: Initial computerised tomography (CT) head imaging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head were unremarkable. Specifically, CT did not identify any orbital pathology. MRI of the orbits revealed bilateral effusions in the suprachoroidal space, with low signal intensity on T1 sequences (Figure 1) and high-intensity signal on T2 sequences (Figure 2). Findings were more pronounced on the right side. Effusions extended anteriorly past the ora serrata. Contrast-enhanced sequences with fat suppression visualised choroidal and scleral enhancement (Figure 3). There was no evidence of intraocular mass or retinal detachment.
 
 A subsequent high-resolution CT chest scan revealed biapical scarring, mild bronchial wall thickening and subtle fissure nodularity in the upper zones (Figure 4) and a 10 mm subcarinal lymph node (Figure 5). Thoracic imaging findings were non-specific and equivocal for sarcoidosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old boy presented with pain over the left hip for the last 3 years and a gradually enlarging mass for the last 2 years. Upon physical examination, the left hip range of motion was reduced. Lab tests showed normal calcium, high serum phosphorous of 7.07 Eq/dL, and normal parathormone levels.
 Imaging Findings: A radiograph of the left hip demonstrated multilobulated periarticular calcifications (Figure 1). MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) showed a well-defined, lobulated lesion affecting the glutei, vastus lateralis, and obturator externus muscles. Multiple fluid-calcium levels, termed the “sedimentation sign”, were noted, with calcifications appearing hypointense on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences, and fluid appearing hypointense on T1-weighted and hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences. These lesions exhibited heterogeneous contrast enhancement. CT imaging revealed a multilobulated calcific mass without bone or joint involvement (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Histopathological examination (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed the diagnosis of tumoral calcinosis, revealing fibro-collagenous tissue with granular calcifications arranged in lobules with surrounding multinucleated giant cells, foamy macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells.

   - Pros: Mass lesion might suggest neoplasm.  
   - Cons: No aggressive bone involvement or invasion; presence of fluid-calcium levels and sedimentation is atypical; calcifications are multilobulated and organized; histology shows inflammatory cells and giant cells, not malignant cells; clinical course is chronic and slow-growing, not typical for sarcoma.

   - Pros: Involves soft tissue muscle; can have calcifications.

   - Pros: Can cause calcifications in soft tissues and joints.

   - Pros: Can cause calcifications in tendons and soft tissues.  
   - Cons: Typically more acute; calcifications are amorphous and less organized; no large multilobulated mass with sedimentation sign; no chronic mass growth; lab findings not typical.

   - Pros: Causes metastatic calcifications and soft tissue calcium deposits.  
   - Cons: Normal parathormone levels; typically no periarticular lobulated calcific masses; lab findings do not support hyperparathyroidism; no bone changes noted.

   - Pros: Classic adolescent presentation with periarticular multilobulated calcific masses; elevated serum phosphate with normal calcium and parathormone; sedimentation sign is characteristic; MRI and CT findings match; histology consistent with tumoral calcinosis.

   - Pros: Can cause periarticular calcifications and joint involvement.  
   - Cons: Typically involves joint space and synovium, not deep muscle bellies; no bone or joint involvement seen; calcifications usually ossified bodies within joint or bursa rather than multilobulated masses with fluid-calcium levels; clinical history and histology do not match.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain, mild jaundice and dark urine for 3 days. Initial blood tests revealed anaemia and elevation of serum creatinine, total bilirubin, and lactate dehydrogenase. Further analytical evaluation excluded autoimmune and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemias. Flow cytometry analysis of peripheral blood showed a deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins. Abdominal ultrasound did not reveal any significant findings, so an abdominal magnetic resonance (MRI) was subsequently conducted.
 Imaging Findings: MRI excluded acute biliary pathology but revealed abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on both T2 (Figure 1) and T1-weighted (Figure 2) sequences. In gradient-recalled echo (GRE) imaging, the renal cortex demonstrated a relative increase in signal intensity on the out-of-phase sequence (Figure 3) compared to the in-phase sequence (Figure 2). Reduced cortical enhancement was observed after intravenous contrast administration (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c). The spleen and liver showed no significant parenchymal signal abnormalities (Figure 5). Isolated renal haemosiderosis was presumed.
 
 Abdominal ultrasound and computed tomography did not reveal significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old male with a history of glioblastoma, submitted to surgical resection followed by Stupp protocol 11 years ago, and organic psychosis, with therapeutic noncompliance and multiple hospitalisations in recent years, presented to the emergency department with acute onset gait disturbance, right-sided muscle weakness, numbness and behavioural changes.
 Imaging Findings: A head CT performed upon admission revealed a status post left parietal craniotomy, with a cortico-subcortical temporoparietal surgical site exhibiting gliosis, stable when compared to previous imaging. No new hypodensities suggestive of stroke were observed, and CT angiography excluded arterial occlusion.
 
 Subsequent brain MRI T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated
 
 de novo
 
 cortical gyriform enhancement in the previously treated surgical site, specifically in the temporoparietal region, with extension into the occipital, insular and remaining temporal lobe. Perfusion arterial spin labelling (ASL) study indicated increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the affected area. Importantly, there were no signs of acute stroke nor tumour progression, as evidenced by the absence of restricted diffusion, an extension of the high T2 FLAIR signal or nodular enhancement.

   - Pros: Classic presentation includes cortical gyriform enhancement involving previously irradiated or glioblastoma resected cortex, especially temporoparietal region; increased perfusion on ASL is commonly seen; symptoms are acute neurological deficits and behavioral changes; no stroke or tumor recurrence.

5) Post-ictal changes  
   - Pros: Can show cortical/subcortical T2/FLAIR changes and sometimes enhancement

   - Pros: Can show cortical/subcortical changes in posterior regions

4) Post-ictal changes – less likely due to enhancement pattern and perfusion findings.  
5) Organic psychosis exacerbation – clinical history present but imaging findings not explained.  
6) Posterior reversible encephalopathy – clinical and imaging mismatch.  
Thus, SMART syndrome is the best match.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 26-year-old female with no relevant past medical history presented with a two-year history of worsening pain at the right proximal humerus and associated weakness.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the right shoulder (Figures 1a and 1b) showed an expansile lucent proximal humeral meta-diaphysis lesion with well-defined medial and craniocaudal margins, narrow zone of transition, mild lateral endosteal scalloping and subtle medial periosteal reaction.
 
 CT of the right shoulder (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a predominantly fat-density soft tissue mass and an extensive area of full-thickness cortical loss anteriorly with focal permeation of the posterior cortex of the meta-diaphysis.
 
 Subsequent MRI (Figures 3, 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) confirmed a 4.8 cm maximum diameter predominantly fat-signal lesion within the proximal humerus with mildly enhancing internal curvilinear soft tissue stranding and more nodular soft tissue superiorly with speckled intra-lesional fat and extensive full thickness cortical loss and medial cortical permeation with secondary stress fracture. Mildly enhancing mild peri-lesional marrow and cortical oedema were also noted. There was no extra-osseous invasion.
 
 Nuclear medicine whole-body scan revealed avid uptake at the right proximal humerus with no other disease.

A) Key findings:

- X-ray: Expansile lucent lesion in proximal humeral meta-diaphysis; well-defined margins medial and craniocaudal, narrow zone of transition; mild lateral endosteal scalloping and subtle medial periosteal reaction.
- CT: Predominantly fat-density soft tissue mass; extensive full-thickness cortical loss anteriorly; focal permeation of posterior cortex; cortical destruction.
- MRI: 4.8 cm predominantly fat-signal lesion within proximal humerus; mildly enhancing internal curvilinear soft tissue stranding; nodular soft tissue superiorly with speckled intra-lesional fat; extensive full-thickness cortical loss and medial cortical permeation; secondary stress fracture; mild enhancing peri-lesional marrow and cortical edema; no extra-osseous invasion.
- Nuclear medicine: Avid uptake at the right proximal humerus; no other lesions.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Central/low-grade osteosarcoma:
   Pros: Cortical permeation and some periosteal reaction; mild marrow and cortical edema enhancing; pain and weakness; location fits.
   Cons: Lesion is predominantly fat-density on CT and MRI, which is unusual for osteosarcoma; osteosarcomas typically are osteoid matrix producing, not fat density; no extra-osseous soft tissue mass; no classic sunburst or Codman triangle.

2) Giant cell tumour:
   Pros: Occurs in young adults; can cause cortical thinning and breakthrough; pain and weakness typical.
   Cons: Lesion usually lytic, expansile, but not predominantly fat density; GCTs often do not have such extensive fat content; usually epiphyseal-located; no internal fat signal.

3) Aneurysmal bone cyst:
   Pros: Expansile lucent lesion; cortical thinning; can have internal septations.
   Cons: Typically blood-filled cystic spaces, not fat density; no internal fat signal; usually multiloculated fluid-fluid levels; no soft tissue invasion; less common in this age group and site.

4) Metastasis:
   Pros: Lytic lesions and cortical destruction possible; avid uptake on nuclear scan.
   Cons: Patient age young; no primary cancer history; lesion predominantly fat density unusual for metastasis; no multiple lesions.

5) Liposarcoma:
   Pros: Fat density lesion; soft tissue invasion possible.
   Cons: Very rare primary intra-osseous liposarcoma; lesion is bone-based with cortical permeation, but liposarcomas are typically extraosseous; liposarcoma in bone rare; no extra-osseous invasion seen.

6) Fibrous dysplasia:
   Pros: Usually affects humerus; expansile lucent lesion.
   Cons: Typically ground-glass matrix; no predominant fat density; no cortical perforation usually; not aggressive; no soft tissue component or periosteal reaction.

C) Ranking:
1) Central/low-grade osteosarcoma - best fits due to cortical permeation, periosteal reaction, marrow edema, and nuclear uptake despite fat density.
2) Liposarcoma - fat density lesion but unlikely primary bone liposarcoma.
3) Giant cell tumour - cortical involvement but no fat signal.
4) Aneurysmal bone cyst - typical multilocular cystic not fat.
5) Metastasis - unlikely at age and no systemic disease.
6) Fibrous dysplasia - imaging not typical.

Thus, central/low-grade osteosarcoma is most consistent despite unusual fat density that may represent fat-containing necrotic tumor or sampling variation.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-month-old girl was brought to casualty with a history of intermittent fever for 1 month, abdominal distension, and straining while passing urine and stools for 4 days. On examination, a soft, tender, non-mobile midline mass was palpated in the suprapubic region.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal greyscale ultrasound revealed a large, well-defined cystic lesion in the pelvis with dense mobile echoes (Figure 1). The mass was seen posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum and was extending into the abdominal cavity up to the umbilicus. The cystic structure was in continuity with the stretched uterine cavity (Figure 2). Suprapubic probe tenderness was positive. There was no evidence of any solid component or calcifications within. Both the ovaries were seen separately from this lesion. Bilateral kidneys showed mild hydronephrosis. Bowel loops were normal in course, calibre, and peristalsis and were displaced to the periphery. On transperineal ultrasound examination, there was a thin membrane of 1 mm thickness at the vaginal orifice (Figure 3).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 3-month-old infant with intermittent fever, abdominal distension, and difficulty with urination and bowel movements.  
- Physical exam: soft, tender, non-mobile midline suprapubic mass.  
- Ultrasound: large, well-defined cystic pelvic lesion with dense mobile echoes, extending from pelvis to umbilicus, situated posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum.  
- Continuity of cystic structure with stretched uterine cavity.  
- No solid components or calcifications.  
- Bilateral ovaries visualized separately from the cystic lesion.  
- Bilateral mild hydronephrosis.  
- Bowel loops normal but displaced peripherally.  
- Transperineal ultrasound showed a thin 1 mm membrane at the vaginal orifice.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Pyometrocolpos  
   - Pro: Large cystic pelvic mass; history of urinary and bowel symptoms due to mass effect; continuity with uterine cavity; presence of a thin vaginal membrane causing obstruction; infant age matches typical presentation.  
   - Con: No explicit mention of purulent fluid or infection signs, but clinical history of fever supports possible infection.  

2) Hematometrocolpos  
   - Pro: Large cystic mass in uterus; can present in newborns/infants.  
   - Con: Hematometrocolpos typically involves hemorrhagic fluid (hypoechoic, no mobile echoes); no history of bleeding disorder; dense mobile echoes less typical for blood.  

3) Hydrometrocolpos  
   - Pro: Large fluid-filled cystic mass connected to uterine cavity; obstruction by vaginal membrane fits classic scenario; infant age; typical ultrasound appearance (anechoic or low-level echoes).  
   - Con: Dense mobile echoes noted which is a bit unusual but can be seen if infected with debris or pus; clinical fever supports infection (pyometrocolpos).  

4) Distended urinary bladder  
   - Pro: Suprapubic mass; bladder distension possible.  
   - Con: Lesion extends to umbilicus and is posterior to the bladder; continuity with uterine cavity; bladder distension would not show continuity with uterus; no mention of bladder wall thickening or usual bladder echo pattern; also presence of vaginal membrane excludes this.  

5) Ovarian neoplasm  
   - Pro: Cystic mass possible in ovaries.  
   - Con: Ovaries seen separately from lesion; mass not originating from ovary; mass continuity with uterus; no solid components or calcifications; age less typical for ovarian tumors; lesion extending into pelvis and umbilicus not typical for ovarian origin.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- Pyometrocolpos and Hydrometrocolpos are close contenders due to large cystic pelvic mass connected to uterine cavity and vaginal obstruction.  
- Key differentiator: clinical history includes intermittent fever (infection) and ultrasound shows dense mobile echoes (suggestive of pus or infected debris). Thin membrane at vaginal orifice also matches obstructive cause of fluid accumulation.  
- Therefore, pyometrocolpos (infected hydrocolpos) best matches the combination of infection signs and imaging features.  

</analysis><final>Pyometrocolpos</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A middle-aged patient presented with what were initially suspected to be new-onset migraines but then developed auditory and visual hallucinations and complex paranoid beliefs. They became confused and intermittently incoherent with deteriorating coordination, becoming unsteady and sustaining several falls. The clinical picture was further complicated by the onset of myoclonus and a rapidly progressive cognitive decline, which raised suspicion for a neurodegenerative condition.
 Imaging Findings: The patient initially underwent a CT which showed only generalised cerebral atrophy.
 
 They then had further investigations with an MRI, including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) images. This revealed widespread restricted diffusion involving almost the entire cortex except the superior temporal gyrus and also affecting the caudate bilaterally and the left lentiform nuclei. Fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) imaging showed a more subtle high signal in these areas. Other than some small and non-specific foci of high signal in the deep white matter, there were no further positive findings.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Hypoxic encephalopathy:  
  Pros: Diffuse cortical restricted diffusion can be seen in hypoxic injury.  
  Cons: Typically involves watershed zones, basal ganglia may be affected but pattern often more symmetric and periventricular; clinically, patient did not have a known hypoxic event; involvement of deep gray matter is common but cortical DWI restriction tends to be more patchy and not as widespread.

- Hyperammonaemia (Hepatic encephalopathy):  
  Pros: Can cause diffuse cerebral edema and diffusion abnormalities.  
  Cons: Usually shows diffuse cortical and white matter changes, but widespread cortical restricted diffusion is unusual; also, basal ganglia involvement is atypical; clinical presentation differs (more delirium, asterixis, less prominent myoclonus or cognitive decline in this rapid time frame).

- Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy:  
  Pros: Can cause cortical and deep gray matter restricted diffusion.  
  Cons: Typically symmetric involvement of cortex and basal ganglia, often involves posterior cortex preferentially; clinical picture would involve hypoglycemia episodes, which were not described.

- Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD):  
  Pros: Classic clinical features match (rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, psychiatric symptoms including hallucinations and paranoia, deteriorating coordination). Imaging shows widespread cortical ribboning on DWI and basal ganglia restricted diffusion, which is characteristic. Superior temporal gyrus sparing is a known pattern in sCJD. FLAIR changes subtle but supportive.  
  Cons: None significant; findings and clinical picture are highly suggestive.

- Encephalitis:  
  Pros: Can cause cortical restricted diffusion and basal ganglia involvement.  
  Cons: Usually presents with fever, acute onset, no widespread cortical ribboning pattern; no mention of infection signs or CSF results; pattern less typical.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD) – Best match for clinical and imaging findings, pathognomonic DWI pattern with cortical ribboning and basal ganglia involvement, typical clinical presentation.  
2) Hypoxic encephalopathy – less likely due to absent hypoxic event and atypical pattern.  
3) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy – less likely due to distribution and clinical context.  
4) Hyperammonaemia – not typical imaging or clinical presentation.  
5) Encephalitis – less likely due to lack of infection signs and imaging distribution.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old woman presented with worsening perineal pain and pustular skin lesions. Examination revealed extensive erythematous and pustular lesions in the perineal and perianal regions, with rope-like dermal thickening and induration. Multiple fistulous openings with purulent and haemoserous discharge were noted.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the perineal and perianal regions revealed dermal and hypodermal band-like structures indicative of fistulas/tunnels, with multiple dermal collections around hair follicles containing hyperechoic hair fragments (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). Some hypodermal fluid collections were connected to the epidermis via sinus tracts (Figure 1d). Power Doppler showed increased vascularity, suggesting active inflammation (Figure 1e).
 
 MRI demonstrated skin thickening at the perineum and labia majora, hypertrophic scarring, and deep subcutaneous fistulas/tunnels extending into the ischioanal space and perianal region (Figures 2a and 2b), with left adductor muscle involvement suggestive of myositis (Figure 2c). The tunnels exhibited post-contrast rim enhancement, indicating active inflammation, but no fistulas communicating with the anal canal lumen were identified.
 
 After immunomodulatory therapy, significant lesion reduction was observed, with resolution of the fistulas and myositis. Residual skin thickening and scarring were noted, with minimal post-gadolinium enhancement, indicating minor active inflammation (Figures 3a and 3b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old male presented to the outpatient clinic with swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, which progressively grew after a fall four months before. He had a previous history of neoadjuvant radiotherapy followed by resection of a soft tissue sarcoma in the left thigh seven years before.
 Imaging Findings: On the preoperative MRI performed seven years before (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 2c), an intramuscular lobulated mass was seen in the left tensor fascia lata, extending into the subcutis. The mass showed areas of high signal on T1WI (Figure 2a), which became hypo-intense after fat suppression (Figure 2b). The mass was heterogeneous, predominantly hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 2b) and showed internal areas of heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium contrast (Figure 2c).
 
 The current MR study revealed a thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, with heterogeneous high signal on T1WI (Figures 3a and 3b) and T2WI (Figures 4a and 4b), persisting after fat suppression. Multiple internal fluid-fluid levels were present. While post-contrast T1WI sequences (Figure 5) showed intralesional heterogeneous high signal, the dynamic subtraction sequence (Figure 6), only showed a thick peripheral enhancing rim. Therefore, the pseudoenhancement seen on the static sequences was due to a haemorrhage-related high native T1WI signal.

- Post-contrast static T1WI shows heterogeneous high signal, but dynamic subtraction sequence shows only a thick peripheral enhancing rim; static high signal due to pseudoenhancement from hemorrhage-related T1 hyperintensity.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Morel-Lavallée lesion in a patient with previous myxoid liposarcoma  
- Pros: Fluid-fluid levels, hemorrhagic content (T1 high signal persisting after fat saturation), history of trauma (fall) â classic for Morel-Lavallée lesions (post-traumatic closed degloving injuries); thick-walled lesion with peripheral enhancement only on dynamic subtraction is typical.  
- Cons: Lesion on contralateral side; previous sarcoma on opposite side, but not a strict contraindication.  
2) Metastasis of myxoid liposarcoma  
- Pros: History of myxoid liposarcoma; potential for metastasis.  
- Cons: No internal solid enhancing components, no nodular or infiltrative patterns; lesions are usually not fluid-fluid levels and not hemorrhagic in pattern. Typically appear as solid masses with enhancement, not as cystic or fluid-fluid level lesions. Also, metastasis would likely be in deep tissues or lungs rather than superficial subcutaneous tissue.  
3) Angiosarcoma  
- Pros: Hemorrhagic nature of lesion could be angiosarcoma.  
- Cons: Typically more infiltrative with solid components and heterogeneous enhancement; angiosarcoma usually shows irregular margins and solid enhancement. Fluid-fluid levels are rare. The lesion is well-defined with peripheral rim enhancement only.  
4) Haematoma  
- Pros: Hemorrhagic lesion with high T1 signal persisting after fat sat; trauma history (fall); fluid-fluid levels consistent with different stages of blood products.  
- Cons: Hematomas usually do not develop thick enhancing walls. However, subacute or chronic hematomas can have thick capsular enhancement. The lesion is well-defined and with peripheral enhancement only, which can be seen in organizing hematoma. Nonetheless, a hematoma alone might not have a thick well-defined wall or a large pseudocapsule unless chronic.  

C) Ranking and selection:  
1) Morel-Lavallée lesion: best fits the findings (post-traumatic hemorrhagic collection with fluid-fluid levels, peripheral rim enhancement only, chronic thick-walled capsule).  
2) Haematoma: close second, but lacks the well-defined thick capsule and rim enhancement pattern typical for Morel-Lavallée injuries.  
3) Angiosarcoma: less likely due to absence of solid enhancement and infiltrative features.  
4) Metastasis: less likely given imaging characteristics inconsistent with typical metastases.  

Conclusion: The imaging features are classic for a Morel-Lavallée lesion, especially considering the trauma history, fluid-fluid levels, and peripheral rim enhancement only with pseudoenhancement on static images due to hemorrhage.  

</analysis>  
<final>Morel-Lavallée lesion in a patient with previous myxoid liposarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old female, with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion, had a CT head and cervical spine in 2007 for unrelated symptoms. Incidentally, imaging identified a heavily calcified lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The patient remained asymptomatic and, therefore, opted for regular follow-up imaging as opposed to surgical management.
 Imaging Findings: The CT brain demonstrated a 2.5 cm (maximal diameter), highly calcified intra-axial lesion with no surrounding oedema or mass effect, within the right cerebellar fossa (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 The MRI demonstrated the same lesion within the right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring 2.5 cm (maximal axial measurement). The lesion abutted the inferior tentorial surface; however, a broad base against it was not exhibited. There was no notable mass effect or surrounding oedema. On T1 (Figure 3) and T2 (Figure 4) weighted images, the lesion displayed a low signal intensity. Additionally, blooming artefacts were observed on gradient imaging (Figure 5).
 
 During the 17-year follow-up period, the patient exhibited no symptoms. Biennially, MRI heads revealed no change in the lesion size or associated mass effect.

Clinical examination revealed a painless 4 cm thickening lesion of the uvula. Cervical palpation revealed no adenopathy. A biopsy of the lesion was performed.
 Imaging Findings: CT scan revealed the presence of a uvula tumour (Figure 1), a suspicious hepatic lesion and a solid cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas. MRI showed a pancreatic body lesion, measuring 22 mm, with cystic and tissue components, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 2), and a 55 mm heterogeneous hepatic mass, of the segment IV, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 3). There was no dilatation of the pancreatic or biliary ducts. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old female patient presented at the Emergency Department with severe acute abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She also referred cessation of flatus and bowel movements for 4 days. Past medical history is unremarkable. On physical exam, the abdomen was distended and tender with no rebound or guarding.
 Imaging Findings: A plain erect abdominal radiograph revealed the presence of an air-distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant consistent with the inverted cecum, and multiple fluid levels (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) was later performed, with coronal and sagittal reconstructions (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5). On CT images, there was a markedly distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant with air-fluid level, measuring a maximum calliper of 7.5 cm, adjacent to a small blind-ending tubular structure compatible with the ileocecal appendix, consistent with the inverted cecum. A clockwise whirl sign composed of spiralled loops of collapsed cecum and ileum, with twisting of central fatty mesentery with enhancing distended vessels, was present (Figures 4a and 4b). The gradual tapering of the obstructed cecum at the site of the whirl resulted in a stenosis, with the appearance of a bird’s beak (Figure 5). There was also dilation of small bowel loops, filled with fluid. There were no signs of bowel perforation, as well as no signs of pneumatosis intestinalis or other signs of vascular compromise.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Our patient is a 64-year-old male admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to a prolonged cardiac arrest, who was on mechanical circulatory support with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). He was COVID-19-negative and presented with an encephalopathic syndrome.
 Imaging Findings: A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed, and the susceptibility-weighted imaging sequence (SWI), revealed multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs). These lesions were diffusely distributed across the subcortical white matter, splenium of the corpus callosum and posterior fossa, affecting the brainstem (midbrain and pons), as well as both cerebellar hemispheres. The cortex and periventricular regions were spared (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). In addition, we found small watershed infarctions (Figures 2a and 2b).

   - Pros: Classic pattern of cerebral microbleeds in critical illness (subcortical white matter, corpus callosum, brainstem, cerebellum) sparing cortex and periventricular white matter fits well; small watershed infarctions support vascular compromise typical in critical illness; patient is critically ill (cardiac arrest, ECMO).  
   - Cons: None significant; fits perfectly.  

2) "Hypertensive encephalopathy":  
   - Pros: Can cause microbleeds and infarctions.  
   - Cons: Usually microbleeds in basal ganglia, thalami, pons, cerebellum often paramedian; also typically chronic hypertension with deep white matter and basal ganglia involvement; no mention of hypertension history; distribution here is atypical (sparing periventricular and deep gray nuclei).

   - Pros: Causes microbleeds in cortex and subcortical areas.  
   - Cons: Usually microbleeds are lobar, cortical/subcortical, sparing deep structures; patient is 64 (some risk but less common); pattern here involves corpus callosum, brainstem, cerebellum which is atypical for CAA; no mention of age-typical CAA features.  

4) "Fat-emboli":  
   - Pros: Can cause cerebral microbleeds and white matter injury.  
   - Cons: Typically associated with long bone fractures or orthopedic surgery; no mention of trauma; microbleeds are usually more diffuse and may show other embolic features; distribution here is typical for critical illness rather than fat embolism.  

5) "COVID-19":  
   - Pros: COVID can cause cerebral microbleeds and vascular lesions.  
   - Cons: Patient is COVID-negative; distribution does not match typical COVID encephalopathy microbleeds (often juxtacortical, frontal lobes); no infection history.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram)
 
 : Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein (Figure 3a), superficial and common femoral veins (Figure 3b) with heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels (Figure 3c).
 
 CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb)
 
 : A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion (Figure 4a) in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein (Figure 4b), superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins (Figure 4c).
 
 CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram)
 
 : Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries (Figure 1a), extending to the right interlobar artery (Figure 1b), right basal trunk (Figure 1c), and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments (Figure 2).
 
 PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography)
 
 : High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion (Figure 5b) and in the deep venous tumour thrombus (Figure 5a), bilateral pulmonary arteries (Figure 6b), right atrium (Figure 6a) and ventricle.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram): Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein, superficial and common femoral veins with a heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels. CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb): A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein, superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins. CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram): Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries, extending to the right interlobar artery, right basal trunk, and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments. PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography): High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion and in the deep venous tumour thrombus, bilateral pulmonary arteries, right atrium and ventricle.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma, Slow flow vascular malformation, Leiomyosarcoma, Vascular leiomyomatosis, Tumour thrombus, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young adult female (20 years old) with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain.  
- Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) from the left popliteal vein extending through the femoral veins into the common iliac vein, associated with a heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels.  
- Imaging shows a lobulated, heterogeneous enhancing mass in intramuscular and intermuscular planes of proximal leg and distal thigh, invading multiple veins (popliteal, superficial femoral, common femoral, and common iliac).  
- CTPA reveals extensive pulmonary artery involvement with tumor emboli (eccentric filling defects) and a thrombus in the right atrium and ventricle.  
- Multiple cavitary lung consolidations likely related to tumor emboli.  
- PET-CT shows high metabolic activity (high SUV) in the left thigh lesion, in the venous tumor thrombus, pulmonary arteries, and cardiac chambers.  
- The lesion is intravascular and involves large central veins and cardiac chambers with metastasis to lungs (via tumor emboli).  
- The lesion is causing venous obstruction and has invaded the venous system extensively.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma  
   - Pros: Synovial sarcoma can occur in young adults, and rare intravascular cases have been reported.  
   - Cons: No mention of characteristic imaging features of synovial sarcoma (e.g., calcifications, solid-cystic components typical of soft tissue synovial sarcoma). Also, the intravascular location with tumor thrombus invading heart chambers and pulmonary arteries fits better with other entities.  

2) Slow flow vascular malformation  
   - Pros: The initial diagnosis suspected this, especially with heteroechoic lesion around vessels and intramuscular locations. Slow flow malformations can mimic venous thrombosis and may appear as heteroechoic lesions.  
   - Cons: The lesion is heterogeneously enhancing, infiltrative, causing intravascular tumor thrombus, PET shows high metabolic activity (malignant), not consistent with benign vascular malformation. Also, tumor emboli and cardiac involvement are not features of vascular malformations.  

3) Leiomyosarcoma  
   - Pros: Leiomyosarcoma is a malignant smooth muscle tumor that commonly involves large veins and can cause intravascular tumor thrombus; it can extend in venous system and produce tumor emboli to lungs and heart; more common in middle-aged women but the features fit.  
   - Cons: The patient is young (20 years old), which is less common but not impossible. Usually arises in uterine or other smooth muscle structures, but primary intravascular leiomyosarcoma, especially in the venous system, is a known entity.  

4) Vascular leiomyomatosis  
   - Pros: Vascular leiomyomatosis (intravenous leiomyomatosis) is a benign (histologically) smooth muscle tumor that extends intravascularly from pelvic veins into the IVC and heart; can cause intravascular tumors and right-heart prolapse; rare, but the intravascular component is characteristic.  
   - Cons: Typically occurs in women of reproductive age with uterine leiomyomas; not commonly associated with pulmonary artery tumor emboli or lung metastases, and PET-CT shows high metabolic activity which is unusual for benign leiomyomatosis (though rare cases might have higher uptake, but less likely). Also, there is no mention of uterine origin.  

5) Tumour thrombus  
   - Pros: The description of tumor thrombus (metabolically active intravascular lesion, pulmonary artery tumor emboli, cardiac chamber involvement) fits "tumor thrombus" as a concept, which is more of a descriptive term rather than a specific pathology.  
   - Cons: "Tumor thrombus" is not a specific diagnosis but a feature seen in many malignancies; needs underlying primary tumor to classify.  

6) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Lymphoma can involve veins and cause thrombus.  
   - Cons: Usually lymphomatous masses are soft tissue or nodal, not intravascular masses extending into cardiac chambers and causing tumor emboli; also, lymphoma less likely to show intravascular intramuscular mass with high SUV in veins plus tumor emboli pattern.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma - possible but less pathognomonic for extensive intravascular mass and tumor emboli.  
2) Leiomyosarcoma - fits more classic morphology/intravascular nature, tumor thrombus, high SUV, pulmonary arteries involvement.  
3) Vascular leiomyomatosis - usually benign, limited to uterine origin and venous spread; not usually high metabolic activity or pulmonary emboli.  
4) Slow flow vascular malformation - rejected based on PET and tumor emboli findings.  
5) Lymphoma - less likely given intravascular and tumor emboli features.  
6) Tumour thrombus - descriptive, not a primary pathology.  

Choosing between primary intravascular synovial sarcoma vs leiomyosarcoma: Leiomyosarcoma more frequently invades veins, causes tumor emboli to lung and heart, and shows high metabolism. Synovial sarcoma can be intravascular but less commonly causes this pattern.  
Thus, the best diagnosis is leiomyosarcoma involving venous system with tumor thrombus and emboli.  

<final>Leiomyosarcoma</final><|return|>
options: Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma | Slow flow vascular malformation | Leiomyosarcoma | Vascular leiomyomatosis | Tumour thrombus | Lymphoma

Clinical History: A 26-year-old male with severe haemophilia A presented to the emergency department with a month-long history of haematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain. The patient is an active smoker and reported a weight loss of 20 kg over the last year. The patient admitted recreational use of ketamine.
 Imaging Findings: Initial ultrasound imaging revealed a low bladder volume with symmetrical bladder wall thickening (Figure 1a) and bilateral hydronephrosis.
 
 CT intravenous urography (IVU) demonstrated mucosal enhancement and confirmed symmetrical bladder wall thickening, forming the classic “hourglass bladder deformity” (Figures 1b and 1c). Additionally, ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis were observed (Figure 2).
