OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male presented with worsening lower back pain for 6 months. On physical examination there was no deformity or neurological deficits noted. There was no history of fever, night sweats or weight loss.
 Imaging Findings: An anteroposterior (AP) and lateral radiograph (Figures 1a and 1b) of the lumbar spine was done initially. It showed reduced intervertebral disc space at the L2–L3 level with adjacent end plate erosions and sclerosis, and soft tissue opacity in the paravertebral region at the L2–L3 lumbar vertebral level. Loss of the psoas muscle outline was also noted.
 
 On MRI, partial destruction of the posteroinferior aspect of L1 and posterosuperior aspect of L2 is seen with involvement of the intervening IV disc (Figures 2a and 2b). Altered signal intensity is seen in the vertebral bodies of L4 and L5, which appear hypointense on T1-weighted (T1WI) and hyperintense on T2-weighted (T2WI). There is an associated extensive, multiloculated prevertebral and bilateral paravertebral collection seen (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). The soft tissue components appear hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2W images. These show peripheral restriction of diffusion (Figures 7a and 7b). On post-contrast imaging (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), there is enhancement of the soft tissue components with central necrotic areas and the involved parts of L1 and L2 vertebral bodies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old female presented to the emergency department with acute lower abdominal pain, accompanied by chronic burning sensations and stabbing pain in the right lower leg. Based on the initial clinical presentation, diverticulitis was suspected, and a routine abdominal CT scan was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT imaging demonstrated a lipomatous, highly vascularised mass located posterior to the proximal femoral diaphysis of the right hip, with extension into the ischiofemoral space (Figures 1a and 1b). The lesion was predominantly composed of fatty tissue and exhibited intense arterial enhancement, consistent with a hypervascular process.
 
 Subsequent MRI offered further characterisation, identifying a complex intramuscular mass within the right quadratus femoris muscle, composed of both lipomatous (hyperintense on T1-weighted images) and nodular components (Figure 2). The nodular areas appeared hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2 fat-suppressed sequences (Figure 3). These regions demonstrated moderate, heterogeneous contrast enhancement, indicating variable internal vascularity (Figure 4). There were no significant signal voids on T2* sequences, effectively excluding haemorrhage or calcification. The lesion did not invade surrounding structures. However, the adjacent sciatic nerve exhibited focal thickening, which was suggestive of possible irritation due to mass effect or local inflammation (Figure 5).

   - Cons: No calcification/ossification seen; T2* shows no signal voids; no peripheral calcification or mature bone; lesion is fatty rather than ossified.

   - Pros: Intramuscular fatty mass.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female patient with right lower limb shortening since childhood was evaluated in consultation for limb lengthening surgery. Her personal history was negative for trauma. On physical examination, she had a clinically measured leg-length discrepancy of 4 cm.
 Imaging Findings: Full-length standing anteroposterior view radiograph (Figure 1a) demonstrated right lower limb shortening secondary to femoral hypoplasia, with normal ossification and no proximal deformity, consistent with Paley type 1A congenital femoral deficiency (CFD). Mild proximal fibular shortening was also noted, compatible with fibular hemimelia (Achterman–Kalamchi type IA).
 
 On the magnified AP radiograph of the right knee (Figure 1b), the femoral intercondylar notch appeared shallow, and the tibial spines were markedly hypoplastic.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur (anterior drawer sign) and uncovering of the lateral meniscus posterior horn (Figure 2b), findings suggestive of anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) insufficiency. On mid-sagittal fat-suppressed proton-density (PD) and oblique sagittal PD-weighted images (Figures 2c and 2d), the ACL was not visualised. The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) was present and vertically oriented (positive PCL line sign). No bone marrow oedema was seen, and the menisci were unremarkable.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old diabetic man, with 25 years of bilateral lower limb weakness, presented with a 1.5-month history of a mildly painful, firm, non-tender swelling over the left shoulder. Limited shoulder movement, reduced sensation, and a scar noted (Figures 1a and 1b). No trauma or aspiration records.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the left shoulder (Figure 2) showed joint destruction, including humeral head and glenoid resorption, widened joint space, periarticular calcifications, and soft tissue inflammation.
 
 MRI of the left shoulder (Figures 4, 5, 6, 7a, and 7b) revealed extensive bony damage, complete tears in the rotator cuff tendons and complete destruction of the glenoid labrum, glenohumeral ligaments, and biceps-labral anchor. A large, thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement extended into the intermuscular and intramuscular planes of the rotator cuff muscles.
 
 MRI of the spine (Figures 3a and 3b) showed a syrinx extending from the cervico-medullary junction to the thoracic vertebra at T11, consistent with syringomyelia, and a wedge collapse of the vertebral body of T12 thoracic vertebra, indicating chronic vertebral involvement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old male presented with severe triventricular ventriculomegaly and cortical thinning at 35 weeks on prenatal ultrasound. At birth, he had an unusual phenotype, adducted thumbs and poor spontaneous mobility. Progressive ventriculomegaly required ventriculoperitoneal derivation (VPD). Currently, the patient has severe neurological involvement, flaccid paresis with paroxysms of rigidity and epilepsy.
 Imaging Findings: Transfontanelar US and postnatal MRI at 1 day and 1 week, respectively, show severe triventricular hydrocephaly and large massa intermedia (Figures 1a and 1b), aqueductal stenosis, enlarged quadrigeminal plate, corpus callosum agenesis, vermian hypoplasia, severe cortical thinning and diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d).
 
 The latest MRI, at the age of 3 years, before VPD treatment and mild improvement of ventriculomegaly, demonstrates better visualisation of the previous findings (Figure 3a), in addition to diffuse hypoplasia of cerebral white matter, polymicrogyria (Figures 3b and 3c), diencephalosynapsis (Figure 3d) with diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia in a so-called “butterfly-like pattern” (Figures 3e and 3f), and hypoplastic and poorly differentiated hippocampi.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male with epigastric pain primarily localised to the right upper quadrant, a positive Murphy’s sign, associated with vomiting and hypotension. The patient presented with a fever spike of 38.1ºC as the only infectious clinical sign observed. Among the analytical parameters, there is a marked elevation of C-reactive protein (CRP; 12.8 mg/dL), procalcitonin (29.8 ng/mL), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH; 2488 U/L), and leukocytosis (24×10^9/L) with neutrophilia (18.9×10^9/L). The family reports diarrhoea in the preceding days.
 Imaging Findings: In a portable chest x-ray, a small left pleural effusion and the presence of gas in the right upper quadrant are seen (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominopelvic CT scan was performed with intravenous contrast in the arterial and portal venous phases. The main findings include numerous subcentimeter gas bubbles arranged in two clusters, primarily affecting the right liver lobe, with maximum axial diameters of 80 mm and 69 mm. Free gas bubbles within the parenchyma and perfusion abnormalities in the affected segments are also observed, along with predominantly central periportal oedema (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 3).
PostDescription:

The family reports diarrhoea in the preceding days.
 Imaging Findings: In a portable chest x-ray, a small left pleural effusion and the presence of gas in the right upper quadrant are seen. Free gas bubbles within the parenchyma and perfusion abnormalities in the affected segments are also observed, along with predominantly central periportal oedema.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Emphysematous hepatitis, Pyogenic liver abscess, Hepatic infarction
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 55-year-old male with right upper quadrant pain, positive Murphy's sign, vomiting, hypotension, fever 38.1Â°C.  
- Laboratory: markedly elevated CRP, procalcitonin (29.8 ng/mL), LDH, leukocytosis with neutrophilia.  
- Clinical context: recent diarrhoea (possible source of infection or endotoxemia).  
- Imaging: portable chest x-ray shows small left pleural effusion and gas in right upper quadrant.  
- Abdominal CT with contrast shows numerous subcentimeter gas bubbles in two clusters in the right liver lobe (largest 80 mm and 69 mm), free gas bubbles in liver parenchyma, perfusion abnormalities in affected segments, and periportal edema (predominantly central).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Emphysematous hepatitis:  
Pros: Presence of extensive intraparenchymal gas bubbles, perfusion abnormalities, and periportal edema are characteristic. The clinical picture with sepsis, rapid deterioration, and gastrointestinal symptoms (post-diarrhoea) supports this diagnosis.  
Cons: Emphysematous hepatitis is rare and severe, but findings and clinical context strongly support it.  

2) Pyogenic liver abscess:  
Pros: Fever, elevated inflammatory markers, leukocytosis and right upper quadrant pain are consistent. Liver abscess may show gas if gas-forming organisms are involved.  
Cons: Usually presents as a larger, well-circumscribed hypodense lesion, often not as multiple small gas bubbles in clusters; abscesses often cause mass effect and rim enhancement. Also, pleural effusion typically ipsilateral. Gas distribution and distribution pattern less typical.  

3) Hepatic infarction:  
Pros: Perfusion abnormality could suggest infarction; hypodense areas without enhancement.  
Cons: Gas formation is not typical of hepatic infarction; usually caused by vascular occlusion, no free gas in parenchyma; clinical sepsis and gas bubbles do not support infarction alone.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Emphysematous hepatitis: most consistent with gas in liver parenchyma, multiple small gas bubbles, perfusion defects, periportal edema, rapid clinical deterioration.  
2) Pyogenic liver abscess: possible but less likely because of imaging pattern and presence of free gas bubbles.  
3) Hepatic infarction: least likely due to presence of gas and sepsis.  
Therefore, the best diagnosis is emphysematous hepatitis (gas-forming infection of the liver parenchyma).  
</analysis>  
<final>Emphysematous hepatitis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 22-year-old female presented with hallux valgus deformity of both first toes since birth (Figure 1). At the age of 12, she began experiencing difficulty walking and progressive joint stiffness. On physical examination, there was an inability to fully open the temporomandibular joints bilaterally and restricted flexion and abduction of the extremities.
 Imaging Findings: On plain radiographs of the feet, there was absence of the distal phalanges of the first toes and hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°, supporting early skeletal deformities (Figure 2). CT imaging of the cervicothoracic spine demonstrated heterotopic ossification of the supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments (Figure 3). Three-dimensional reconstructions showed multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between the iliac bones and the twelfth ribs, as well as ossified extensions from the right iliac bone to the right humerus (Figures 4a and 4b).

 Imaging Findings: On plain radiographs of the feet, there was absence of the distal phalanges of the first toes and hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°, supporting early skeletal deformities. CT imaging of the cervicothoracic spine demonstrated heterotopic ossification of the supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments. Three-dimensional reconstructions showed multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between the iliac bones and the twelfth ribs, as well as ossified extensions from the right iliac bone to the right humerus.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH), Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans, Ankylosing spondylitis
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 22-year-old female with bilateral hallux valgus deformity since birth and absence of distal phalanges of first toes.  
- Progressive joint stiffness and difficulty walking since age 12, with restricted temporomandibular joint motion.  
- CT: heterotopic ossification of supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments in cervicothoracic spine.  
- 3D reconstruction showing multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between iliac bones and twelfth ribs, and between right iliac bone and right humerus.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH):  
   Pros: Ossification of spinal ligaments and contiguous flowing ossifications on imaging are classic for DISH. Involves anterior longitudinal ligament and tends to affect older adults, leading to flowing ossifications along vertebral bodies.  
   Cons: DISH typically spares sacroiliac joints and does not cause heterotopic ossification involving soft tissue like ligaments in a bridging tubular fashion between distant bones (e.g., pelvis to ribs or humerus); also, it does not cause distal phalanx absence or hallux valgus from birth; age is much younger than typical DISH presentation.  

2. Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans (likely referring to Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva or FOP):  
   Pros: Classic features include progressive heterotopic ossification of soft tissues, especially in connective tissues like muscles and ligaments. Presence of ossification bridging pelvis to ribs and limb bones (iliac to humerus) is typical. Restricted neck and temporomandibular joint motion and early skeletal deformities (e.g., hallux valgus, absent distal phalanges of great toes) are hallmarks of FOP, which often presents with congenital hallux valgus or malformed great toes.  
   Cons: None significant; findings align well with known FOP presentation.  

3. Ankylosing spondylitis:  
   Pros: Can cause spinal ligament ossifications and ankylosis.  
   Cons: Typically affects sacroiliac joints early, not sparing them; usually presents in young adults but not with heterotopic ossification bridging distant soft tissues like pelvis to humerus; does not cause distal phalangeal absences or congenital hallux valgus; temporomandibular involvement is uncommon.  

C) Ranking:  
1. Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans (i.e., Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva) – strong pathognomonic features (congenital hallux valgus with distal phalanx absence, progressive heterotopic ossification bridging distant bones, soft tissue ligament ossification, facial/jaw joint restriction).  
2. Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis – less fitting due to age, pattern of ossification, and congenital foot deformities.  
3. Ankylosing spondylitis – least fitting given congenital aspects and heterotopic ossification pattern.  

</analysis>  
<final> Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old patient with no notable personal or family medical history. The patient has been complaining for the past 3 years of bilateral gonalgia, right shoulder pain, and rachialgia, with more significant functional discomfort in the lower limbs. Clinical examination reveals tenderness upon palpation of the knees and right shoulder.
 Imaging Findings: A radiological assessment was performed (x-rays and full-body scan) revealing multiple exostotic lesions of the dorsolumbar spine, sternoclavicular joints, costal arches, scapulas, as well as in bones of the upper limbs (bilaterally, more marked at the metaphyseal-epiphyseal region of the right proximal humerus), pelvic girdle, and bones of the lower limbs and feet (bilaterally, more marked at the knees).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 5-year-old girl presented with a progressively enlarging occipital swelling and mild pain. She had prior right-eye surgeries for eyelid coloboma, with corneal opacification of uncertain cause. She had no light perception in the right eye and reduced vision in the left. No systemic issues. Siblings were healthy.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a 2.8 cm bony defect at the posterior fontanelle, through which a partially calcified lipomatous lesion was herniating into the subcutaneous scalp (Figure 1a). This lesion extended intracranially along the posterior falx, causing indentation of the superior sagittal sinus (Figure 1b). A second linear lipoma (10 × 2 mm) was seen at the splenium of the corpus callosum (Figure 1c). A 3D volume-rendered reconstruction of the cranial defect shows the 2.8 cm bony defect at the posterior fontanelle (Figure 1d).
 
 MRI confirmed T1-hyperintense lipomas at the posterior fontanelle and splenium, with an extracranial component (Figure 2a). T2-weighted MRI revealed an arachnoid cyst at the left cerebellopontine angle (Figure 2b), and confirmed bilateral colobomas and absence of the right lens (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old male presented to the emergency department at our institution with the chief complaints of insidious, throbbing frontal headache, dizziness, photo-phonophobia and emesis for one week. Neurological examination revealed a positive Kernig sign, ataxia and impaired finger-to-nose testing.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction.
 
 Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. There was no evident enlargement of supratentorial ventricles or periventricular oedema. We observed an inferior cerebellar tonsillar descent of 7 mm.
 
 CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old male presented to the emergency department at our institution with the chief complaints of insidious, throbbing frontal headache, dizziness, photo-phonophobia and emesis for one week.  Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations. The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction. Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst. Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. There was no evident enlargement of supratentorial ventricles or periventricular oedema. We observed an inferior cerebellar tonsillar descent of 7 mm. CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma, Pilocytic astrocytoma, Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma, MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion), Oligodendroglioma-like tumour

A) Key findings:

- Mass effect: mild perilesional edema, partial effacement of fourth ventricle, tonsillar descent 7 mm  
- CT: thick, linear striated calcifications with “tiger-striped” appearance  
- Bony remodeling with scalloping of occipital bone suggesting indolent lesion  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma (Lhermitte-Duclos disease):  
   - Pros: classic finding of striated T2 dark bands ("tiger-striped") on MRI; lesion causing cerebellar folia expansion; typically a mixed cystic-solid lesion; usually occurs in cerebellum; can cause hydrocephalus; indolent nature with bony remodeling.  
   - Cons: Usually in children and young adults; often presents with cerebellar signs; pathognomonic thickened cerebellar folia and striated T2 signal; no enhancement is common, but enhancement can be variable.  

2) Pilocytic astrocytoma:  
   - Pros: Common in children; often cystic with mural nodule; commonly in cerebellum; enhancement of mural nodule.  
   - Cons: Usually shows cystic lesion with mural nodule rather than diffuse folial involvement; calcifications are uncommon; bony remodeling less typical.  

3) Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma:  
   - Pros: Mixed solid-cystic lesions; can show enhancement; can occur in cerebellum.  
   - Cons: Usually does not show the "tiger-striped" calcifications or striated T2 dark bands; typical imaging less characteristic; bony remodeling less common.  

4) MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion):  
   - Pros: Molecular diagnosis; may be related to low-grade gliomas.  
   - Cons: Not a distinct imaging diagnosis; molecular classification; imaging not specific; no unique imaging features described.  

5) Oligodendroglioma-like tumour:  
   - Pros: Oligodendrogliomas can have calcifications and cysts; usually supratentorial location.  
   - Cons: Rare in cerebellum; usually in adults; imaging location not typical; bony remodeling unlikely.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma (Lhermitte-Duclos disease) – classic "tiger-striped" T2 hypointense bands, location, and bony remodeling strongly support this diagnosis.  
2) Pilocytic astrocytoma – second because it is a common pediatric cerebellar tumor but lacks the specific striated calcifications and bony changes.  
3) Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma – less specific imaging features.  
4) Oligodendroglioma-like tumour – unlikely due to location and age.  
5) MAP kinase pathway altered – molecular subclass but no specific imaging pattern given.  

Given that the patient is a child with a cerebellar lesion showing classic "tiger-striped" T2 hypointense bands and thick linear calcifications on CT, along with bony remodeling, the most consistent diagnosis is dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma (Lhermitte-Duclos disease).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 45-year-old gentleman presented in the neurology department with a 6-month history of neurocognitive decline with behavioural and personality changes associated with myoclonus and gait imbalance.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain demonstrated asymmetrical areas of high signal in DWI and T2/FLAIR with corresponding normal to low signal in ADC along the cortical convexities of the right frontoparieto-temporal lobes and the right parasagittal region (Figures 1a, 1b, 2a, and 2b). Bilateral symmetric high signal in DWI and T2/FLAIR with corresponding low signal in ADC are seen in the bilateral caudate nucleus, globus pallidus and bilateral posteromedial thalamus (Figures 3a, 3b, and 4).
PostDescription:

Bilateral symmetric high signal in DWI and T2/FLAIR with corresponding low signal in ADC are seen in the bilateral caudate nucleus, globus pallidus and bilateral posteromedial thalamus.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old woman taking etanercept for psoriatic arthritis presented to the emergency department with pain in her right wrist for three weeks, predominantly in the fourth and fifth fingers. She reported no history of trauma or prior procedures. Clinical examination revealed swelling on the palmar surface and pain with movement. An ultrasound was performed, which showed synovial fluid (images not available).
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the right hand showed fluid in the synovial sheath of the flexor tendons (Figure 1a), extending from the distal radioulnar joint to the metacarpals, with multiple small nodules within the tendon sheath, ranging from hypointense to isointense, consistent with “rice bodies” (Figures 1b, 1c, and 1d). Soft tissue enlargement and thickening of the flexor sheath tendons were also observed (Figures 1e and 1f).
 
 Two months after starting treatment, a follow-up MRI was performed, which showed the disappearance of the “rice bodies” with a slight persistence of synovial fluid in the flexor tendon sheath (Figures 2a and 2b). A decrease in soft tissue enlargement and flexor sheath thickening was also observed (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 35-year-old gentleman presented with a gradually progressive headache since 6 months, which was eventually accompanied by visual disturbance, giddiness and difficulty in walking. There was no history of trauma or any other systemic complaints. Fundoscopic examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced MRI brain revealed a 3.1 x 3.4 x 3.5 cm-sized T2 hypointense (Figure 1), and thick-walled, ring-enhancing (Figure 2) lesion in the left hemicerebellum. The lesion showed T1 isointense signal (Figure 3) without any diffusion restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). Associated, there was significant perilesional oedema and mass effect causing compression of the fourth ventricle (Figure 1) with resultant moderate upstream hydrocephalus and periventricular cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) seepage. On MR spectroscopy, the lesion showed a prominent lipid lactate peak with reduced choline and N-acetylaspartate levels (Figures 5a and 5b).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Age and clinical presentation: 35-year-old male with subacute symptoms of headache, visual disturbances, giddiness, and gait difficulties. No trauma or systemic complaints, fundoscope normal (no papilledema).  
- MRI Brain: Contrast-enhancing MRI shows a solitary lesion in the left hemicerebellum measuring around 3.1 x 3.4 x 3.5 cm, with T2 hypointense signal and a thick-walled, ring enhancement pattern.  
- T1 isointense signal without diffusion restriction (no restricted diffusion on DWI).  
- Significant perilesional edema and mass effect with hydrocephalus and periventricular CSF seepage due to fourth ventricle compression.  
- MR spectroscopy: prominent lipid-lactate peak, reduced choline and NAA peaks.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Glioma  
   - Pros: Can cause mass effect, edema, and hydrocephalus; can present as ring-enhancing lesion in posterior fossa; T2 hypointensity can be seen in some gliomas due to dense cellularity or calcification.  
   - Cons: Typically show elevated choline on MRS, not reduced; usually no prominent lipid-lactate peak unless necrosis; usually more heterogeneous on T1 and T2; no diffusion restriction expected (fits), but overall gliomas rarely show thick ring enhancement in posterior fossa in young adults without systemic signs.  

2) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can show restricted diffusion (but here no diffusion restriction), ring enhancement is rare but can happen; hyperdense on CT, iso/hypointense on T2.  
   - Cons: Lymphomas usually show elevated choline, reduced NAA, no lactate/lipid peak unless necrotic; usually no perilesional edema; location less common in posterior fossa; usually diffusion restriction present (here absent).  

3) Posterior fossa metastases  
   - Pros: Ring-enhancing lesions with perilesional edema common; metastases can involve cerebellum; can cause hydrocephalus and mass effect.  
   - Cons: Patient is young with no known primary cancer; MR spectroscopy showing lipid-lactate peak more typical for necrotic/caseating lesions rather than metastases; lactate/lipid can appear but usually not the hallmark; no systemic symptoms.  

4) Giant caseating cerebellar tuberculoma  
   - Pros: Classical ring-enhancement with thick wall in posterior fossa; T2 hypointensity expected due to caseous necrosis; MR spectroscopy showing prominent lipid-lactate peak is characteristic of tuberculomas with caseation; no diffusion restriction consistent with caseating solid tuberculomas; clinical history compatible with subacute/subchronic infection without systemic signs.  
   - Cons: No fever or systemic TB signs, but isolated cerebellar tuberculomas can occur; fundoscopic exam normal fits with no papilledema despite hydrocephalus (can happen).  

5) Pyogenic abscess  
   - Pros: Ring-enhancing lesion with diffusion restriction (abscess core) typically seen; edema and mass effect common.  
   - Cons: No diffusion restriction here; T2 hypointensity uncommon (usually T2 hyperintense with central diffusion restriction); MR spectroscopy with lactate and lipid possible but often shows elevated amino acids, acetate, succinate; clinical absence of systemic infection; fundoscopic normal.  

6) Mycotic abscess  
   - Pros: Similar to pyogenic abscess ring-enhancement; can cause edema and hydrocephalus.  
   - Cons: Typically show diffusion restriction, T2 hyperintensity; no diffusion restriction here; clinical history does not support immunosuppression or systemic fungal infection; MR spectroscopy features not typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1. Giant caseating cerebellar tuberculoma (fits MRI signal characteristics, MR spectroscopy findings, ring enhancement, no diffusion restriction, and perilesional edema).  
2. Glioma (possible but less likely due to spectroscopy and lack of elevated choline).  
3. Posterior fossa metastases (less likely due to no systemic signs).  
4. Lymphoma (less likely due to lack of diffusion restriction and spectroscopy profile).  
5. Pyogenic or mycotic abscess (excluded mainly due to lack of diffusion restriction and T2 hypointensity).  

Thus, the most compatible diagnosis is Giant caseating cerebellar tuberculoma.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Giant caseating cerebellar tuberculoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 An 8-year-old boy presented with recurrent focal seizures since infancy and progressive developmental delay, along with a port-wine stain on the right side of his face, present since birth.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 54-year-old male with a right hemifacial port-wine stain presented with long-standing epilepsy, progressive left-hemiplegia, cognitive decline and migraine-like headaches. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed visual field defects and glaucoma.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Non-contrast CT images of the brain revealed gyriform, curvilinear cortical and subcortical calcifications along the right parieto-occipital region, with a “tram-track” appearance and associated mild cortical atrophy (Figure 1a). Post-contrast CT images demonstrated pial enhancement over the right parieto-occipital cortex, consistent with pial angiomas. There was also enlargement and enhancement of the ipsilateral choroid plexus (Figure 1b). Additionally, homogeneous crescentic enhancement of the choroid was found along the posterior segment of the right globe, consistent with a choroidal angioma (Figure 1c).
 
 Case 2
 
 T2-weighted and FLAIR axial MR images show right cerebral volume loss with diffusely thinned cortex, ex vacuo dilatation of ipsilateral lateral ventricle, and extensive curvilinear cortical hypointensity (Figures 2a and 2b). Axial susceptibility-weighted image (SWI) shows “blooming” of the extensive cortical/subcortical calcifications on the right cerebral hemisphere (Figure 2c). Post-contrast T1-weighted image shows serpentine gyriform enhancement, and enlargement and enhancement of choroid plexus (Figure 2d).
PostDescription:
Clinical History:
 Case 1: An 8-year-old boy presented with recurrent focal seizures since infancy and progressive developmental delay, along with a port-wine stain on the right side of his face, present since birth.
 Case 2: A 54-year-old male with a right hemifacial port-wine stain presented with long-standing epilepsy, progressive left-hemiplegia, cognitive decline and migraine-like headaches. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed visual field defects and glaucoma.
 
 Imaging Findings:
 Case 1: Non-contrast CT images of the brain revealed gyriform, curvilinear cortical and subcortical calcifications along the right parieto-occipital region, with a “tram-track” appearance and associated mild cortical atrophy. Post-contrast CT images demonstrated pial enhancement over the right parieto-occipital cortex, consistent with pial angiomas. There was also enlargement and enhancement of the ipsilateral choroid plexus. Additionally, homogeneous crescentic enhancement of the choroid was found along the posterior segment of the right globe, consistent with a choroidal angioma.
 Case 2: T2-weighted and FLAIR axial MR images show right cerebral volume loss with diffusely thinned cortex, ex vacuo dilatation of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle, and extensive curvilinear cortical hypointensity. Axial susceptibility-weighted image shows “blooming” of the extensive cortical/subcortical calcifications on the right cerebral hemisphere. Post-contrast T1-weighted image shows serpentine gyriform enhancement, and enlargement and enhancement of the choroid plexus.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Meningioangiomatosis, Blue rubber bleb nevus syndrome, Wyburn-Mason syndrome, Sturge–Weber syndrome, type 1, PHACE syndrome
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Both cases have port-wine stains on one side of the face (right side in both).  
- Neurologic symptoms: seizures, developmental delay (case 1), progressive hemiplegia, cognitive decline, and vision issues (case 2).  
- Imaging: gyriform, curvilinear cortical and subcortical calcifications in the right hemisphere (parieto-occipital in case 1, diffuse right hemisphere in case 2) with a "tram-track" appearance; choroid plexus enlargement and enhancement ipsilateral to the brain findings; post-contrast pial or serpentine gyriform enhancement consistent with pial angiomas; choroidal angioma (case 1); MR susceptibility-weighted imaging shows blooming of calcifications.  
- Imaging clearly demonstrates leptomeningeal angiomas and choroidal angioma.  
- There is cerebral atrophy and ex vacuo dilatation of the lateral ventricle on the affected side.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1. Meningioangiomatosis  
   - Pros: Can present with calcifications and seizures.  
   - Cons: Usually affects cortex but does not have a typical port-wine stain, leptomeningeal angioma, or choroidal angioma; no vascular malformations or choroid plexus enlargement typical of meningioangiomatosis.  
   - Verdict: Does NOT explain port-wine stain or choroidal angioma.  

2. Blue rubber bleb nevus syndrome  
   - Pros: Can have venous malformations.  
   - Cons: Typically involves multiple venous malformations in skin and viscera, no port-wine stains, no leptomeningeal angioma or choroidal angioma; neuroimaging findings not typical.  
   - Verdict: No fit with imaging or clinical signs.  

3. Wyburn-Mason syndrome  
   - Pros: Arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) of the brain and retina.  
   - Cons: No port-wine stain; usually high-flow AVMs, no leptomeningeal angiomas or choroidal angiomas described; clinical presentation and imaging do not match.  
   - Verdict: Inconsistent with port-wine stain and leptomeningeal/calcification findings.  

4. Sturge–Weber syndrome, type 1  
   - Pros: Classic triad includes facial port-wine stain (usually in V1/V2 distribution), leptomeningeal angiomas with calcifications and cortical atrophy, choroidal angioma; seizures, hemiparesis, cognitive deficits, and visual disturbances are common; imaging findings fit perfectly (tram-track calcifications, pial enhancement).  
   - Cons: None.  

5. PHACE syndrome  
   - Pros: Hemangiomas of the face, posterior fossa anomalies, arterial anomalies, cardiac defects, and eye anomalies.  
   - Cons: Typically presents in infants with skin hemangiomas, not a port-wine stain; no leptomeningeal angiomas or calcifications as described; this syndrome does not match the imaging findings.  
   - Verdict: Does not fit imaging and clinical picture.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1. Sturge–Weber syndrome, type 1 (perfect match to classic triad and imaging findings).  
2. Wyburn-Mason syndrome (vascular involvement but no port-wine stain or leptomeningeal angiomas).  
3. Meningioangiomatosis (calcifications and seizures but no vascular features or port-wine stain).  
4. PHACE syndrome (vascular and ocular anomalies but imaging mismatch).  
5. Blue rubber bleb nevus syndrome (no fit with imaging or clinical context).  

<final>Sturge–Weber syndrome, type 1</final><|return|>
options: Meningioangiomatosis | Blue rubber bleb nevus syndrome | Wyburn-Mason syndrome | Sturge–Weber syndrome | type 1 | PHACE syndrome

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 : A 9-year-old girl presented to the otorhinolaryngology (ENT) department with profound hearing loss. On evaluation, she was detected to have right-sided sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL).
 
 Case 2
 
 : A 6-year-old boy was referred from the ENT department with a clinical diagnosis of severe right-sided SNHL.
 
 Case 3
 
 : A 2-year-old child was referred with a clinical diagnosis of right-sided SNHL for MRI.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain were performed, and the most relevant findings for each case are noted below:
 
 Case 1
 
 : Thinned-out cochlear division of the right vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII) (Figure 1b). Stenosed right internal auditory meatus (IAM) (Figure 1a). The facial nerve CN VII appeared normal.
 
 Case 2
 
 : Non-visualised cochlear division of the right CN VIII (Figure 2a). CN VII appeared normal.
 
 Case 3
 
 : The cochlear nerve is not visualised in the anteroinferior aspect of the right IAM (Figure 3a).

- MRI findings consistently show abnormalities of the right vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII), specifically the cochlear division.
- Case 1: Thinned-out cochlear division of right CN VIII, stenosed right internal auditory meatus (IAM); CN VII normal.
- Case 2: Non-visualised cochlear division of right CN VIII; CN VII normal.
- Case 3: Cochlear nerve not visualised in right IAM anteroinferior aspect.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Cochlear nerve deficiency
   - Pros: Directly corresponds to findings of absent, thinned, or non-visualised cochlear nerve/nervus cochlearis on MRI. Fits well with SNHL and described nerve findings.
   - Cons: None apparent; fully matches imaging and clinical history.

2) Auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder
   - Pros: Clinical presentation of SNHL.
   - Cons: This is typically a functional disorder without necessarily anatomic nerve absence or deformation; imaging often normal or shows subtle changes, not nerve absence/stenosis. Imaging findings in these cases are structural nerve abnormalities, not just functional impairment.

3) Mondini dysplasia
   - Pros: Can cause SNHL.
   - Cons: This is an inner ear malformation (cochlear and vestibular labyrinth malformation). Imaging findings here focus on nerve absence or thinning, not cochlear malformation. No mention of inner ear malformation or cochlear hypoplasia.

4) Acoustic neuroma
   - Pros: Involves CN VIII and internal auditory meatus.
   - Cons: Acoustic neuroma is a tumor; imaging would show a mass lesion enhancing mass in IAM or cerebellopontine angle. Here, no mass is described; rather there is absence/stenosis, not enlargement.

C) Ranking:
1) Cochlear nerve deficiency: Best fit due to MRI evidence of absent or thinned cochlear nerve component of CN VIII and associated internal auditory meatus stenosis, directly explaining SNHL.
2) Auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder: Less likely because imaging does not show functional disorder but structural nerve absence.
3) Mondini dysplasia: Not supported by findings (no structural inner ear malformation described).
4) Acoustic neuroma: Excluded due to absence of a mass lesion.

Conclusion: The imaging findings and clinical presentation best fit cochlear nerve deficiency.
